title: São Paulo

infobox.value: [Manuel da Nóbrega](/wiki/Manuel_da_N%C3%B3brega "Manuel da Nóbrega") and [Joseph of Anchieta](/wiki/Joseph_of_Anchieta "Joseph of Anchieta")

[Kingdom of Portugal](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Portugal "Kingdom of Portugal") [United Kingdom of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Portugal,_Brazil_and_the_Algarves "United Kingdom of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves") [Empire of Brazil](/wiki/Empire_of_Brazil "Empire of Brazil")
[Municipal Chamber of São Paulo](/wiki/Municipal_Chamber_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Municipal Chamber of São Paulo")

[1st](/wiki/List_of_largest_cities_in_the_Americas "List of largest cities in the Americas") in the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas") [1st](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Brazil_by_population "List of cities in Brazil by population") in Brazil

infobox.key: • [Metropolitan Region of São Paulo](/wiki/Greater_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Greater São Paulo")(2024)

infobox.value: ([/ˌsaʊ(m) ˈpaʊloʊ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"),[[11]](#cite_note-11) Portuguese: [[sɐ̃w ˈpawlu]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Br-SaoPaulo.ogg "File:Br-SaoPaulo.ogg"); [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") for '[Saint Paul](/wiki/Paul_the_Apostle "Paul the Apostle")') is the [capital city](/wiki/Capital_city "Capital city") of the [state of São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_(state) "São Paulo (state)"), as well as the [most populous city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Brazil_by_population "List of cities in Brazil by population") in [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") and [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America"). The city exerts international influence in commerce, finance, culture, gastronomy, arts, fashion, technology, entertainment and media, having been listed by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO")'s [Creative Cities Network](/wiki/Creative_Cities_Network "Creative Cities Network") as a "[City of Film](/wiki/City_of_Film "City of Film")" and the title of "World Capital of Gastronomy", and by the [Globalization and World Cities Research Network](/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network "Globalization and World Cities Research Network") (GaWC) as an [alpha global city](/wiki/Global_city "Global city").[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) It is the [largest urban area by population outside Asia](/wiki/List_of_largest_cities#List "List of largest cities") and the most populous [Portuguese-speaking city](/wiki/Geographical_distribution_of_Portuguese_speakers "Geographical distribution of Portuguese speakers") in the world. The city's name "São Paulo" honors [Paul the Apostle](/wiki/Paul_the_Apostle "Paul the Apostle") and people from the city are known as *paulistanos*. The city's [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") motto is *Non ducor, duco*, which translates as "I am not led, I lead".[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Most populous city in Brazil

**São Paulo** ([/ˌsaʊ(m) ˈpaʊloʊ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"),[[11]](#cite_note-11) Portuguese: [[sɐ̃w ˈpawlu]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Br-SaoPaulo.ogg "File:Br-SaoPaulo.ogg"); [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") for '[Saint Paul](/wiki/Paul_the_Apostle "Paul the Apostle")') is the [capital city](/wiki/Capital_city "Capital city") of the [state of São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_(state) "São Paulo (state)"), as well as the [most populous city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Brazil_by_population "List of cities in Brazil by population") in [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") and [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America"). The city exerts international influence in commerce, finance, culture, gastronomy, arts, fashion, technology, entertainment and media, having been listed by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO")'s [Creative Cities Network](/wiki/Creative_Cities_Network "Creative Cities Network") as a "[City of Film](/wiki/City_of_Film "City of Film")" and the title of "World Capital of Gastronomy", and by the [Globalization and World Cities Research Network](/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network "Globalization and World Cities Research Network") (GaWC) as an [alpha global city](/wiki/Global_city "Global city").[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) It is the [largest urban area by population outside Asia](/wiki/List_of_largest_cities#List "List of largest cities") and the most populous [Portuguese-speaking city](/wiki/Geographical_distribution_of_Portuguese_speakers "Geographical distribution of Portuguese speakers") in the world. The city's name "São Paulo" honors [Paul the Apostle](/wiki/Paul_the_Apostle "Paul the Apostle") and people from the city are known as *paulistanos*. The city's [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") motto is *Non ducor, duco*, which translates as "I am not led, I lead".[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Founded in 1554 by [Jesuit priests](/wiki/Jesuits "Jesuits"), São Paulo is one of the [oldest continuously inhabited cities](/wiki/List_of_oldest_continuously_inhabited_cities#South_America "List of oldest continuously inhabited cities") on the [American continent](/wiki/Americas "Americas"). It played a strategic role during the [Brazilian colonial period](/wiki/Colonial_Brazil "Colonial Brazil"), serving as the center and starting point for the expeditions of the [Paulista *bandeirantes*](/wiki/Bandeirantes "Bandeirantes") (pioneers) settlers for the territorial and economic expansion of the country; however, its economic opulence was consolidated during the [Brazilian coffee cycle](/wiki/Brazilian_coffee_cycle "Brazilian coffee cycle") in the mid-19th century. From that period onward, it became the stage for landmark events in [Brazilian history](/wiki/History_of_Brazil "History of Brazil"), such as the [Cry of Ipiranga](/wiki/Independence_of_Brazil "Independence of Brazil"), the [Modern Art Week](/wiki/Modern_Art_Week "Modern Art Week"), the [1932 Revolution](/wiki/Constitutionalist_Revolution "Constitutionalist Revolution"), and the [Diretas Já](/wiki/Diretas_J%C3%A1 "Diretas Já") ([Direct Elections Now](/wiki/Direct_election "Direct election")) movement. In the 20th century, it consolidated its role as the main national economic hub with [industrialization in Brazil](/wiki/Industry_in_Brazil "Industry in Brazil"), which made the city a [cosmopolitan](/wiki/Multiculturalism "Multiculturalism") [melting pot](/wiki/Melting_pot "Melting pot"), home to the largest [Arab](/wiki/Arab_Brazilians "Arab Brazilians"), [Italian](/wiki/Italian_Brazilians "Italian Brazilians"), and [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_Brazilians "Japanese Brazilians") diasporas in the world, with [ethnic neighborhoods](/wiki/Ethnic_enclave "Ethnic enclave") like [Bixiga](/wiki/Bixiga "Bixiga"), [Bom Retiro](/wiki/Bom_Retiro_(district_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo) "Bom Retiro (district of São Paulo)"), and [Liberdade](/wiki/Liberdade_(district_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo) "Liberdade (district of São Paulo)"), and people from more than 200 other countries.[[17]](#cite_note-Imigrantes2016-17) The city's [metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area"), [Greater São Paulo](/wiki/Greater_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Greater São Paulo"), is home to more than 20 million inhabitants and ranks as the [most populous in Brazil](/wiki/List_of_metropolitan_areas_in_Brazil "List of metropolitan areas in Brazil") and one of the [most populous in the world](/wiki/List_of_largest_cities#List "List of largest cities"). The process of [conurbation](/wiki/Conurbation "Conurbation") between the metropolitan areas around Greater São Paulo also created the [São Paulo Macrometropolis](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_macrometropolis "São Paulo macrometropolis"),[[18]](#cite_note-18) the first [megalopolis](/wiki/Megalopolis "Megalopolis") in the [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere"), with more than 30 million inhabitants.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-Megalópole-20)

São Paulo is the largest [urban economy](/wiki/Urban_economics "Urban economics") in [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") and [one of the world's major financial centres](/wiki/Global_Financial_Centres_Index "Global Financial Centres Index"),[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) representing around 10% of the [Brazilian GDP](/wiki/Economy_of_Brazil "Economy of Brazil")[[23]](#cite_note-Brazilian_GDP-23) and just over a third of [São Paulo state's GDP](/wiki/Economy_of_the_state_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Economy of the state of São Paulo") (the only Brazilian city to surpass the 1 trillion [reais](/wiki/Brazilian_real "Brazilian real") mark in GDP).[[24]](#cite_note-24) The city is the headquarters of [B3](/wiki/B3_(stock_exchange) "B3 (stock exchange)"), the [largest stock exchange of Latin America by market capitalization](/wiki/List_of_major_stock_exchanges "List of major stock exchanges"),[[25]](#cite_note-B3-25) and has several [financial districts](/wiki/List_of_financial_districts "List of financial districts"), mainly in the areas around [Paulista](/wiki/Paulista_Avenue "Paulista Avenue"), [Faria Lima](/wiki/Brigadeiro_Faria_Lima_Avenue "Brigadeiro Faria Lima Avenue") and [Berrini](/wiki/Engenheiro_Lu%C3%ADs_Carlos_Berrini_Avenue "Engenheiro Luís Carlos Berrini Avenue") avenues. Home to 63% of established [multinationals](/wiki/Multinational_corporation "Multinational corporation") in Brazil[[26]](#cite_note-Cidade_do_Mundo-26) and the source of around one third of the Brazilian scientific production,[[27]](#cite_note-27) São Paulo is among the top 50 science and technology clusters in the world.[[28]](#cite_note-28) Its main university, the [University of São Paulo](/wiki/University_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "University of São Paulo"), is often considered [the best in Brazil and Latin America](/wiki/College_and_university_rankings "College and university rankings"),[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30) while the city is regularly ranked as one of the best cities in the world to be a university student in the [QS World University Rankings](/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings "QS World University Rankings").[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32) The metropolis is also home to several of [the tallest skyscrapers in Brazil](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Brazil "List of tallest buildings in Brazil"), including the [Alto das Nações](/wiki/Alto_das_Na%C3%A7%C3%B5es "Alto das Nações"), [Platina 220](/wiki/Platina_220 "Platina 220"), [Figueira Altos do Tatuapé](/wiki/Figueira_Altos_do_Tatuap%C3%A9 "Figueira Altos do Tatuapé"), [Mirante do Vale](/wiki/Mirante_do_Vale "Mirante do Vale"), [Edifício Itália](/wiki/Edif%C3%ADcio_It%C3%A1lia "Edifício Itália"), [Altino Arantes Building](/wiki/Altino_Arantes_Building "Altino Arantes Building"), [North Tower](/wiki/Centro_Empresarial_Na%C3%A7%C3%B5es_Unidas "Centro Empresarial Nações Unidas") and [many others](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "List of tallest buildings in São Paulo"). It is the [state capital](/wiki/List_of_capitals_of_subdivisions_of_Brazil "List of capitals of subdivisions of Brazil") with the best [basic sanitation](/wiki/Improved_sanitation "Improved sanitation"),[[33]](#cite_note-33) the second-most developed, according to the [FIRJAN Municipal Development Index](/wiki/Industry_Federation_of_the_State_of_Rio_de_Janeiro "Industry Federation of the State of Rio de Janeiro") (2025),[[34]](#cite_note-34) the sixth in the [Social Progress Index](/wiki/Social_Progress_Index "Social Progress Index") (IPS) in Brazil,[[35]](#cite_note-35) and the ninth most wooded in the country,[[36]](#cite_note-36) which earned it the international titles of "Tree City of the World" from the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") (UN) and "[Ibero-American](/wiki/Ibero-America "Ibero-America") Green Capital",[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38) in addition to recognition from [Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records "Guinness World Records") for the "largest food security program in the world".[[39]](#cite_note-39)

The place of origin and/or disseminator of countless artistic, cultural, and gastronomic expressions, such as the [caipira](/wiki/Caipiras "Caipiras") [culture](/wiki/Caipira_culture "Caipira culture") and cuisine, [sertanejo music](/wiki/Sertanejo_music "Sertanejo music"), Brazilian [hip-hop](/wiki/Brazilian_hip-hop "Brazilian hip-hop") and [rock](/wiki/Brazilian_rock "Brazilian rock"), and [samba paulista](/wiki/Samba_paulista "Samba paulista"), and the culinary matrix of creations like [bauru](/wiki/Bauru_(sandwich) "Bauru (sandwich)"), [beirute](/wiki/Beirute "Beirute"), [calabresa](/wiki/Lingui%C3%A7a_calabresa "Linguiça calabresa"), [coxinha](/wiki/Coxinha "Coxinha"), dadinho, [parmegiana](/wiki/Bife_%C3%A0_parmegiana "Bife à parmegiana"), [pastel de feira](/wiki/Pastel_(Brazilian_food) "Pastel (Brazilian food)"), and [picanha](/wiki/Picanha "Picanha"), the city is one of the main cultural, gastronomic, and entertainment hubs in the world. It is home to monuments, parks, and museums, such as the [Latin American Memorial](/wiki/Latin_America_Memorial "Latin America Memorial"), [Ibirapuera Park](/wiki/Ibirapuera_Park "Ibirapuera Park"), [São Paulo Museum of Art](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Museum_of_Art "São Paulo Museum of Art"), [Pinacoteca](/wiki/Pinacoteca_do_Estado_de_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Pinacoteca do Estado de São Paulo"), [Cinemateca](/wiki/Cinemateca_Brasileira "Cinemateca Brasileira"), [Itaú Cultural](/wiki/Ita%C3%BA_Cultural "Itaú Cultural"), [Museum of Ipiranga](/wiki/Museu_do_Ipiranga "Museu do Ipiranga"), [Catavento Museum](/wiki/Catavento_Museum "Catavento Museum"), [Football Museum](/wiki/Football_Museum "Football Museum"), [Museum of the Portuguese Language](/wiki/Museum_of_the_Portuguese_Language "Museum of the Portuguese Language"), and the [Museum of Image and Sound](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Museum_of_Image_and_Sound "São Paulo Museum of Image and Sound"). São Paulo also holds notable cultural events like the [São Paulo Art Biennial](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Art_Biennial "São Paulo Art Biennial"), [São Paulo Fashion Week](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Fashion_Week "São Paulo Fashion Week"), [Lollapalooza](/wiki/Lollapalooza#Brazil "Lollapalooza"), [Primavera Sound](/wiki/Primavera_Sound#São_Paulo "Primavera Sound"), [Comic Con Experience](/wiki/Comic_Con_Experience "Comic Con Experience") and the [São Paulo Gay Pride Parade](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Gay_Pride_Parade "São Paulo Gay Pride Parade"), the [second-largest LGBT event](/wiki/List_of_largest_LGBTQ_events "List of largest LGBTQ events") in the world after the [New York City Pride March](/wiki/New_York_City_Pride_March "New York City Pride March").[[40]](#cite_note-NYCWorld'sLargestPrideParade-40)[[41]](#cite_note-NYCWorld-41) São Paulo has also hosted many sporting events such as the [1950](/wiki/1950_FIFA_World_Cup "1950 FIFA World Cup") and [2014 FIFA World Cups](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup "2014 FIFA World Cup"), the [1963 Pan American Games](/wiki/1963_Pan_American_Games "1963 Pan American Games"), the [São Paulo Indy 300](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Indy_300 "São Paulo Indy 300") and the [NFL Brazil Games](/wiki/NFL_International_Series#Brazil_Games "NFL International Series"), in addition to hosting the annual [Brazilian Grand Prix](/wiki/Brazilian_Grand_Prix "Brazilian Grand Prix") of [Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One") and the [Saint Silvester Road Race](/wiki/Saint_Silvester_Road_Race "Saint Silvester Road Race").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A3o_Paulo&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the city of São Paulo](/wiki/History_of_the_city_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "History of the city of São Paulo")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of São Paulo](/wiki/Timeline_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Timeline of São Paulo").

### Pre-colonial period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A3o_Paulo&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Pre-colonial period")]

Historical affiliations

> [![Portugal](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Flag_of_Portugal_%281640%29.svg/40px-Flag_of_Portugal_%281640%29.svg.png)](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal") [Portuguese Empire](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire") 1554–1815  
> ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Flag_of_the_United_Kingdom_of_Portugal%2C_Brazil%2C_and_the_Algarves.svg/20px-Flag_of_the_United_Kingdom_of_Portugal%2C_Brazil%2C_and_the_Algarves.svg.png) [United Kingdom of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Portugal,_Brazil_and_the_Algarves "United Kingdom of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves") 1815–1822  
> ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Flag_of_Brazil_%281822%E2%80%931853%29.svg/40px-Flag_of_Brazil_%281822%E2%80%931853%29.svg.png) [Empire of Brazil](/wiki/Empire_of_Brazil "Empire of Brazil") 1822–1889  
> [![Brazil](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/0/05/Flag_of_Brazil.svg/40px-Flag_of_Brazil.svg.png)](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") [Republic of Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") 1889–present

The region of modern-day São Paulo, originally known as Piratininga plains around the [Tietê River](/wiki/Tiet%C3%AA_River "Tietê River"), was inhabited by the [Tupi people](/wiki/Tupi_people "Tupi people"), such as the [Tupiniquim](/wiki/Tupiniquim "Tupiniquim"), Guaianás, and [Guarani](/wiki/Guaran%C3%AD_people "Guaraní people"). Other tribes also lived in areas that today form the metropolitan region.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

The region was divided in Caciquedoms (chiefdoms) at the time of encounter with the Europeans.[[43]](#cite_note-43) The most notable [cacique](/wiki/Cacique "Cacique") was [Tibiriçá](/wiki/Tibiri%C3%A7%C3%A1 "Tibiriçá"), known for his support for the Portuguese and other European colonists. Among the many indigenous names of places, rivers, neighborhoods, etc., that survive today are [Tietê](/wiki/Tiet%C3%AA,_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Tietê, São Paulo"), [Ipiranga](/wiki/Ipiranga_(district_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo) "Ipiranga (district of São Paulo)"), [Tamanduateí](/wiki/Tamanduate%C3%AD_River "Tamanduateí River"), [Anhangabaú](/wiki/Vale_do_Anhangaba%C3%BA "Vale do Anhangabaú"), [Piratininga](/wiki/Piratininga "Piratininga"), [Itaquaquecetuba](/wiki/Itaquaquecetuba "Itaquaquecetuba"), [Cotia](/wiki/Cotia "Cotia"), [Itapevi](/wiki/Itapevi "Itapevi"), [Barueri](/wiki/Barueri "Barueri"), [Embu-Guaçu](/wiki/Embu-Gua%C3%A7u "Embu-Guaçu"), etc.

### Colonial period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A3o_Paulo&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Colonial period")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Oscar_Pereira_da_Silva_-_Funda%C3%A7%C3%A3o_de_S%C3%A3o_Paulo%2C_1909.jpg/250px-Oscar_Pereira_da_Silva_-_Funda%C3%A7%C3%A3o_de_S%C3%A3o_Paulo%2C_1909.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oscar_Pereira_da_Silva_-_Funda%C3%A7%C3%A3o_de_S%C3%A3o_Paulo,_1909.jpg)

*Founding of São Paulo*, 1909 painting by [Oscar Pereira da Silva](/wiki/Oscar_Pereira_da_Silva "Oscar Pereira da Silva")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/P%C3%A1tio_do_Col%C3%A9gio_in_S%C3%A3o_Paulo.jpg/250px-P%C3%A1tio_do_Col%C3%A9gio_in_S%C3%A3o_Paulo.jpg)](/wiki/File:P%C3%A1tio_do_Col%C3%A9gio_in_S%C3%A3o_Paulo.jpg)

Courtyard of the college, *Pátio do Colégio*, in the [Historic Center of São Paulo](/wiki/Historic_Center_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Historic Center of São Paulo"). At this location, the city was founded in 1554. The current building is a reconstruction made in the late 20th century, based on the [Jesuit](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") [college](/wiki/College "College") and [church](/wiki/Church_(building) "Church (building)") that were erected at the site in 1653.

The Portuguese village of [São Paulo dos Campos de Piratininga](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_dos_Campos_de_Piratininga "São Paulo dos Campos de Piratininga") was marked by the founding of the [Colégio de São Paulo de Piratininga](/wiki/P%C3%A1tio_do_Col%C3%A9gio "Pátio do Colégio") on 25 January 1554. The [Jesuit](/wiki/Society_of_Jesus "Society of Jesus") college of twelve priests included [Manuel da Nóbrega](/wiki/Manuel_da_N%C3%B3brega "Manuel da Nóbrega") and Spanish priest [José de Anchieta](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_de_Anchieta "José de Anchieta"). They built a mission on top of a steep hill between the [Anhangabaú](/wiki/Anhangaba%C3%BA_River "Anhangabaú River") and [Tamanduateí](/wiki/Tamanduate%C3%AD_River "Tamanduateí River") rivers.[[44]](#cite_note-Lawrence183-44)

They first had a small structure built of [rammed earth](/wiki/Rammed_earth "Rammed earth"), made by Native Indian workers in their traditional style. The priests wanted to [evangelize](/wiki/Catechesis "Catechesis") these Indians who lived in the Plateau region of Piratininga and convert them to Christianity. The site was separated from the coast by the [Serra do Mar](/wiki/Serra_do_Mar "Serra do Mar") mountain range, called "Serra Paranapiacaba" by the Indians.

The college was named for a Christian saint and its founding on the feast day of the celebration of the conversion of the Apostle [Paul of Tarsus](/wiki/Paul_of_Tarsus "Paul of Tarsus"). Father José de Anchieta wrote this account in a letter to the Society of Jesus:

> The settlement of the region's Courtyard of the College began in 1560. During the visit of [Mem de Sá](/wiki/Mem_de_S%C3%A1 "Mem de Sá"), [Governor-General of Brazil](/wiki/List_of_governors-general_of_Brazil "List of governors-general of Brazil"), the [Captaincy of São Vicente](/wiki/Captaincy_of_S%C3%A3o_Vicente "Captaincy of São Vicente"), he ordered the transfer of the population of the Village of [São Bernardo do Campo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Bernardo_do_Campo "São Bernardo do Campo") to the vicinity of the college. It was then named "College of St. Paul of the Piratininga". The new location was on a steep hill adjacent to a large wetland, the [Várzea do Carmo](/wiki/V%C3%A1rzea_do_Carmo "Várzea do Carmo"). It offered better protection from attacks by local Indian groups. It was renamed Vila de São Paulo, belonging to the Captaincy of São Vicente.

For the next two centuries, São Paulo developed as a poor and isolated village that survived largely through the cultivation of [subsistence crops](/wiki/Subsistence_agriculture "Subsistence agriculture") by the labor of natives. For a long time, São Paulo was the only colonial village in Brazil's interior, as travel was too difficult for many to reach the area. Mem de Sá forbade colonists to use the Caminho do Piraquê *(Piraquê Path)* and today known as [Piaçaguera](/wiki/Pia%C3%A7aguera "Piaçaguera"), because of frequent Indian raids along it.

On 22 March 1681, Luís Álvares de Castro, the Second Marquis de Cascais and donee of the Captaincy of [São Vicente](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Vicente,_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Vicente, São Paulo"), moved the capital to the village of São Paulo *(see [Timeline of São Paulo](/wiki/Timeline_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Timeline of São Paulo"))*, designating it the "Head of the captaincy". The new capital was established on 23 April 1683, with public celebrations.

#### The Bandeirantes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A3o_Paulo&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: The Bandeirantes")]

Main article: [Bandeirantes](/wiki/Bandeirantes "Bandeirantes")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Sao_Paulo_-_Monumento_%C3%A0s_Bandeiras_-_foto_Carlos_Alkmin_nr_07-13_4282b.jpg/250px-Sao_Paulo_-_Monumento_%C3%A0s_Bandeiras_-_foto_Carlos_Alkmin_nr_07-13_4282b.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sao_Paulo_-_Monumento_%C3%A0s_Bandeiras_-_foto_Carlos_Alkmin_nr_07-13_4282b.jpg)

The [Monument to the Bandeiras](/wiki/Monument_to_the_Bandeiras "Monument to the Bandeiras") commemorates the 17th-century *[bandeiras](/wiki/Bandeiras "Bandeiras")*.

In the 17th century, São Paulo was one of the poorest regions of the [Portuguese colony](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire"). It was also the center of interior colonial development. Because they were extremely poor, the Paulistas could not afford to buy African slaves, as did other Portuguese colonists. The discovery of gold in the region of [Minas Gerais](/wiki/Minas_Gerais "Minas Gerais"), in the 1690s, brought attention and new settlers to São Paulo. The Captaincy of São Paulo and Minas de Ouro (see [Captaincies of Brazil](/wiki/Captaincies_of_Brazil "Captaincies of Brazil")) was created on 3 November 1709, when the Portuguese crown purchased the Captaincies of São Paulo and Santo Amaro from the former grantees.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

Conveniently located in the country, up the steep *[Serra do Mar](/wiki/Serra_do_Mar "Serra do Mar")* escarpment/mountain range when traveling from [Santos](/wiki/Santos,_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Santos, São Paulo"), while also not too far from the coastline, São Paulo became a safe place to stay for tired travelers. The town became a center for the *[bandeirantes](/wiki/Bandeirantes "Bandeirantes")*, intrepid invaders who marched into unknown lands in search for gold, diamonds, precious stones, and Indians to enslave. The *bandeirantes*, which could be translated as "flag-bearers" or "flag-followers", organized excursions into the land with the primary purpose of profit and the expansion of territory for the Portuguese crown. Trade grew from the local markets and from providing food and accommodation for explorers. The *bandeirantes* eventually became politically powerful as a group, and forced the expulsion of the [Jesuits](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") from the city of São Paulo in 1640. The two groups had frequently come into conflict because of the Jesuits' opposition to the domestic [slave trade](/wiki/History_of_slavery "History of slavery") in Indians.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Entrada_Leste_de_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_em_1821.jpg/250px-Entrada_Leste_de_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_em_1821.jpg)](/wiki/File:Entrada_Leste_de_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_em_1821.jpg)

East entrance of the city of São Paulo in 1821, by Arnaud Julien Pallière (1784–1862)

On 11 July 1711, the town of São Paulo was elevated to city status. Around the 1720s, gold was found by the pioneers in the regions near what are now [Cuiabá](/wiki/Cuiab%C3%A1 "Cuiabá") and [Goiânia](/wiki/Goi%C3%A2nia "Goiânia"). The Portuguese expanded their Brazilian territory beyond the [Tordesillas Line](/wiki/Tordesillas_Line "Tordesillas Line") to incorporate the gold regions. When the gold ran out in the late 18th century, São Paulo shifted to growing [sugar cane](/wiki/Sugar_cane "Sugar cane"). Cultivation of this commodity crop spread through the interior of the Captaincy. The sugar was exported through the [Port of Santos](/wiki/Port_of_Santos "Port of Santos"). At that time, the first modern highway between São Paulo and the coast was constructed and named the Calçada do Lorena *("Lorena's [settway](/wiki/Sett_(paving) "Sett (paving)")")*. Nowadays, the estate that is home to the Governor of the State of São Paulo, in the city of São Paulo, is called the *[Palácio dos Bandeirantes](/wiki/Pal%C3%A1cio_dos_Bandeirantes "Palácio dos Bandeirantes")* (Bandeirantes Palace), in the neighborhood of [Morumbi](/wiki/Morumbi,_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Morumbi, São Paulo").

### Imperial period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A3o_Paulo&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Imperial period")]

Main article: [Empire of Brazil](/wiki/Empire_of_Brazil "Empire of Brazil")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Vista_a%C3%A9rea_do_Monumento_%C3%A0_Independ%C3%AAncia.jpg/250px-Vista_a%C3%A9rea_do_Monumento_%C3%A0_Independ%C3%AAncia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vista_a%C3%A9rea_do_Monumento_%C3%A0_Independ%C3%AAncia.jpg)

[Monument to Independence](/wiki/Monument_to_the_Independence_of_Brazil "Monument to the Independence of Brazil") in [Independence Park](/wiki/Independence_Park_(S%C3%A3o_Paulo) "Independence Park (São Paulo)"), located at the place where then-[Prince Pedro](/wiki/Pedro_I_of_Brazil "Pedro I of Brazil") proclaimed the [Independence of Brazil](/wiki/Independence_of_Brazil "Independence of Brazil")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Marc_Ferrez_-_IMS_002001MF005003.jpg/250px-Marc_Ferrez_-_IMS_002001MF005003.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marc_Ferrez_-_IMS_002001MF005003.jpg)

[Cathedral Square of São Paulo](/wiki/Pra%C3%A7a_da_S%C3%A9 "Praça da Sé") in 1880, during the reign of [Emperor Pedro II](/wiki/Pedro_II_of_Brazil "Pedro II of Brazil") by [Marc Ferrez](/wiki/Marc_Ferrez_(photographer) "Marc Ferrez (photographer)")

After Brazil became [independent from Portugal](/wiki/Independence_of_Brazil "Independence of Brazil") in 1822, as declared by [Emperor Pedro I](/wiki/Pedro_I_of_Brazil "Pedro I of Brazil") where the [Monument to the Independence of Brazil](/wiki/Monument_to_the_Independence_of_Brazil "Monument to the Independence of Brazil") is located, he named São Paulo as an Imperial City. In 1827, a law school was founded at the Convent of São Francisco, today part of the [University of São Paulo](/wiki/University_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "University of São Paulo"). The influx of students and teachers gave a new impetus to the city's growth, thanks to which the city became the *Imperial City and Borough of Students of St. Paul of Piratininga*.

The [expansion of coffee production](/wiki/Coffee_cycle "Coffee cycle") was a major factor in the growth of São Paulo, as it became the region's chief export crop and yielded good revenue. It was cultivated initially in the [Paraíba Valley](/wiki/Para%C3%ADba_Valley "Paraíba Valley") region in the East of the State of São Paulo, and later on in the regions of [Campinas](/wiki/Campinas "Campinas"), [Rio Claro](/wiki/Rio_Claro,_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Rio Claro, São Paulo"), [São Carlos](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Carlos "São Carlos") and [Ribeirão Preto](/wiki/Ribeir%C3%A3o_Preto "Ribeirão Preto").

From 1869 onward, São Paulo was connected to the port of Santos by the [Estrada de Ferro Santos-Jundiaí](/wiki/Estrada_de_Ferro_Santos-Jundia%C3%AD "Estrada de Ferro Santos-Jundiaí") (Santos-Jundiaí Railroad), nicknamed *The Lady*. By the late 19th century, several other railroads connected the interior to the state capital. São Paulo became the point of convergence of all railroads from the interior of the state. Coffee was the economic engine for major economic and population growth in the State of São Paulo.

In 1888, the "Golden Law" (*[Lei Áurea](/wiki/Lei_%C3%81urea "Lei Áurea")*) was sanctioned by [Isabel, Princess Imperial of Brazil](/wiki/Isabel,_Princess_Imperial_of_Brazil "Isabel, Princess Imperial of Brazil"), abolishing [slavery in Brazil](/wiki/Slavery_in_Brazil "Slavery in Brazil"). Until then, enslaved had been the main source of labor in the coffee plantations. After abolition, and following governmental stimulus towards the increase of immigration, the province began to receive a large number of immigrants, largely Italians, Japanese and Portuguese peasants, many of whom settled in the capital. The region's first industries also began to emerge, providing jobs to the newcomers, especially those who had to learn Portuguese.

### Old Republican period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A3o_Paulo&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Old Republican period")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Guilherme_Gaensly_-_Esta%C3%A7%C3%A3o_da_Luz%2C_c._1900.jpg/250px-Guilherme_Gaensly_-_Esta%C3%A7%C3%A3o_da_Luz%2C_c._1900.jpg)](/wiki/File:Guilherme_Gaensly_-_Esta%C3%A7%C3%A3o_da_Luz,_c._1900.jpg)

[Luz Station](/wiki/Luz_Station "Luz Station") in 1900

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Avenida_Paulista_1902_restored_version.jpg/250px-Avenida_Paulista_1902_restored_version.jpg)](/wiki/File:Avenida_Paulista_1902_restored_version.jpg)

[Paulista Avenue](/wiki/Paulista_Avenue "Paulista Avenue") in 1902

By the time Brazil became a republic on 15 November 1889, coffee exports were still an important part of São Paulo's economy. São Paulo grew strong in the national political scene, taking turns with the also rich state of Minas Gerais in electing [Brazilian presidents](/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_Brazil "List of presidents of Brazil"), an alliance that became known as "[coffee and milk](/wiki/Caf%C3%A9_com_leite_politics "Café com leite politics")", given that [Minas Gerais](/wiki/Minas_Gerais "Minas Gerais") was famous for its dairy production. During this period, São Paulo went from regional center to national metropolis, becoming industrialized and reaching its first million inhabitants in 1928. Its greatest growth in this period was relative in the 1890s when it doubled its population. The height of the coffee period is represented by the construction of the second [Luz Station](/wiki/Luz_Station "Luz Station") (the present building) at the end of the 19th century and by the [Paulista Avenue](/wiki/Paulista_Avenue "Paulista Avenue") in 1900, where they built many mansions.[[46]](#cite_note-Prefeitura-46)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Palacetes_Prates_e_Vale_do_Anhangaba%C3%BA.jpg/250px-Palacetes_Prates_e_Vale_do_Anhangaba%C3%BA.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palacetes_Prates_e_Vale_do_Anhangaba%C3%BA.jpg)

[Viaduto do Chá](/wiki/Viaduto_do_Ch%C3%A1 "Viaduto do Chá") and [Anhangabaú Valley](/wiki/Vale_do_Anhangaba%C3%BA "Vale do Anhangabaú") in the 1920s

Industrialization was the economic cycle that followed the coffee plantation model. By the hands of some industrious families, including many immigrants of Italian and Jewish origin, factories began to arise and São Paulo became known for its smoky, foggy air. The cultural scene followed [modernist](/wiki/Modernism "Modernism") and [naturalist](/wiki/Naturalism_(philosophy) "Naturalism (philosophy)") tendencies in fashion at the beginning of the 20th century. Some examples of notable modernist artists are poets [Mário de Andrade](/wiki/M%C3%A1rio_de_Andrade "Mário de Andrade") and [Oswald de Andrade](/wiki/Oswald_de_Andrade "Oswald de Andrade"), artists [Anita Malfatti](/wiki/Anita_Malfatti "Anita Malfatti"), [Tarsila do Amaral](/wiki/Tarsila_do_Amaral "Tarsila do Amaral") and [Lasar Segall](/wiki/Lasar_Segall "Lasar Segall"), and sculptor [Victor Brecheret](/wiki/Victor_Brecheret "Victor Brecheret"). The [Modern Art Week](/wiki/Modern_Art_Week "Modern Art Week") of 1922 that took place at the [Theatro Municipal](/wiki/Theatro_Municipal_(S%C3%A3o_Paulo) "Theatro Municipal (São Paulo)") was an event marked by [avant-garde](/wiki/Avant-garde "Avant-garde") ideas and works of art. In 1929, São Paulo won its first skyscraper, the [Martinelli Building](/wiki/Martinelli_Building "Martinelli Building").[[46]](#cite_note-Prefeitura-46)

The modifications made in the city by Antônio da Silva Prado, Baron of Duprat and [Washington Luís](/wiki/Washington_Lu%C3%ADs "Washington Luís"), who governed from 1899 to 1919, contributed to the climate development of the city; some scholars consider that the entire city was demolished and rebuilt at that time. São Paulo's main economic activities derive from the services industry – factories are since long gone, and in came financial services institutions, law firms, consulting firms. Old factory buildings and warehouses still dot the landscape in neighborhoods such as [Barra Funda](/wiki/Barra_Funda "Barra Funda") and [Brás](/wiki/Br%C3%A1s "Brás"). Some cities around São Paulo, such as [Diadema](/wiki/Diadema,_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Diadema, São Paulo"), [São Bernardo do Campo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Bernardo_do_Campo "São Bernardo do Campo"), [Santo André](/wiki/Santo_Andr%C3%A9 "Santo André"), and [Cubatão](/wiki/Cubat%C3%A3o "Cubatão") are still heavily industrialized to the present day, with factories producing from cosmetics to chemicals to automobiles.

In 1924 the city was the stage of the [São Paulo Revolt](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Revolt_of_1924 "São Paulo Revolt of 1924"), an armed conflict fought in working-class neighborhoods near the center of São Paulo that lasted 23 days, from 5 to 28 July, leaving hundreds dead and thousands injured. The confrontation between the federal troops of president [Artur Bernardes](/wiki/Artur_Bernardes "Artur Bernardes") against rebels of the [Brazilian Army](/wiki/Brazilian_Army "Brazilian Army") and the [Public Force of São Paulo](/wiki/Public_Forces_(Brazil) "Public Forces (Brazil)") was classified by the federal government as a conspiracy, a mutiny and a "revolt against the Fatherland, without foundation, headed by disorderly members of the Brazilian Army".[[47]](#cite_note-Revolução_de_1924-47) To face the rebels, the federal government launched an [indiscriminate artillery bombardment](/wiki/Bombing_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Bombing of São Paulo") against the city, which affected mostly civilian targets; as a result of the bombing, a third of São Paulo's 700,000 inhabitants fled the city. The revolt has been described as "the largest [urban conflict](/wiki/Urban_combat_in_the_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Revolt_of_1924 "Urban combat in the São Paulo Revolt of 1924") in the history of Brazil".[[48]](#cite_note-48)

### Revolution of 1932 and contemporary era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A3o_Paulo&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Revolution of 1932 and contemporary era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Manifesta%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Pra%C3%A7a_Patriarca_maio_32.jpg/250px-Manifesta%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Pra%C3%A7a_Patriarca_maio_32.jpg)](/wiki/File:Manifesta%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Pra%C3%A7a_Patriarca_maio_32.jpg)

Demonstration in [Patriarca Square](/wiki/Patriarca_Square "Patriarca Square") shortly before the [Constitutionalist Revolution of 1932](/wiki/Constitutionalist_Revolution_of_1932 "Constitutionalist Revolution of 1932")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Werner_Haberkorn_-_Vista_parcial_do_Vale_do_Anhangaba%C3%BA._S%C3%A3o_Paulo-SP_11_restored.jpg/250px-Werner_Haberkorn_-_Vista_parcial_do_Vale_do_Anhangaba%C3%BA._S%C3%A3o_Paulo-SP_11_restored.jpg)](/wiki/File:Werner_Haberkorn_-_Vista_parcial_do_Vale_do_Anhangaba%C3%BA._S%C3%A3o_Paulo-SP_11_restored.jpg)

São Paulo in the 1960s, with the [Martinelli](/wiki/Martinelli_Building "Martinelli Building"), [Altino Arantes](/wiki/Altino_Arantes_Building "Altino Arantes Building") and [Mirante do Vale](/wiki/Mirante_do_Vale "Mirante do Vale") buildings

Main article: [Constitutionalist Revolution](/wiki/Constitutionalist_Revolution "Constitutionalist Revolution")

In 1932, São Paulo mobilized in its largest civic movement: the [Constitutionalist Revolution](/wiki/Constitutionalist_Revolution "Constitutionalist Revolution"), when the entire population engaged in the war against the "Provisional Government" of [Getúlio Vargas](/wiki/Get%C3%BAlio_Vargas "Getúlio Vargas"). In 1934, with the reunion of some faculties created in the 19th century, the [University of São Paulo](/wiki/University_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "University of São Paulo") (USP) was founded, today the largest in Brazil.[[49]](#cite_note-HCSP-49)[[50]](#cite_note-Times-50)

The first major project for industrial installation in the city was the industrial complex of [Indústrias Matarazzo](/wiki/Ind%C3%BAstrias_Reunidas_F%C3%A1bricas_Matarazzo "Indústrias Reunidas Fábricas Matarazzo") in [Barra Funda](/wiki/Barra_Funda "Barra Funda"). In the 1930s, the Jafet brothers, operating in the fabric business, Rodolfo Crespi, the Puglisi Carbone brothers and the Klabin family, who would found the first large cellulose industry in Brazil, the [Klabin](/wiki/Klabin "Klabin").[[51]](#cite_note-51) Another major industrial boom occurred during the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), due to the crisis in coffee farming in the 1930s and restrictions on international trade during the war, which resulted in the city having a very high economic growth rate that remained high in the post-war period.[[52]](#cite_note-SP450-52)

In 1947, São Paulo gained its first paved highway: the [Via Anchieta](/wiki/Via_Anchieta "Via Anchieta") (built on the old route of [José de Anchieta](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_de_Anchieta "José de Anchieta")), connecting the capital to the coast of São Paulo. In the 1950s, São Paulo was known as "the city that never stop" and as "the fastest growing city in the world".[[52]](#cite_note-SP450-52) São Paulo held a large celebration, in 1954, of the "Fourth Centenary" of the city's founding, when the [Ibirapuera Park](/wiki/Ibirapuera_Park "Ibirapuera Park") was inaugurated, many historical books are released and the source of the [Tietê River](/wiki/Tiet%C3%AA_River "Tietê River") in [Salesópolis](/wiki/Sales%C3%B3polis "Salesópolis") is discovered. With the transfer of part of the city's financial center, which was located in the historic center (in the region called the "Historic Triangle"), to [Paulista Avenue](/wiki/Paulista_Avenue "Paulista Avenue"), its mansions were, for the most part, replaced by large buildings.[[52]](#cite_note-SP450-52)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_-_Center.jpg/250px-S%C3%A3o_Paulo_-_Center.jpg)](/wiki/File:S%C3%A3o_Paulo_-_Center.jpg)

Prestes Maia Avenue in 1974

In the period from the 1930s to the 1960s, the great entrepreneurs of São Paulo's development were mayor [Francisco Prestes Maia](/wiki/Francisco_Prestes_Maia "Francisco Prestes Maia") and the governor [Ademar de Barros](/wiki/Ademar_de_Barros "Ademar de Barros"), who was also mayor of São Paulo between 1957 and 1961. Prestes Maia designed and implemented, in the 1930s, the "Avenue Plan for the City of São Paulo", which revolutionized São Paulo's traffic.[[53]](#cite_note-53) These two rulers are also responsible for the two biggest urban interventions, after the Avenues Plan, which changed São Paulo: the [rectification](/wiki/River_engineering "River engineering") of the Tietê river with the construction of its banks and the [São Paulo Metro](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Metro "São Paulo Metro"): on February 13, 1963, governor Ademar de Barros and mayor Prestes Maia created study commissions (state and municipal) to prepare the basic project for the São Paulo Metro, and allocated their first funds to the Metro.[[54]](#cite_note-54) At the beginning of the 1960s, São Paulo already had four million inhabitants. Construction of the metro began in 1968, under the administration of Mayor [José Vicente de Faria Lima](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Vicente_de_Faria_Lima "José Vicente de Faria Lima"), and the commercial operation started on September 14, 1974. In 2016 the system had a network 71.5 km long and 64 stations spread across five lines. That year, 1.1 billion passengers were transported by the system.[[55]](#cite_note-MetroHist-55)

At the end of the 20th century and beginning of the 21st century, São Paulo became the main financial center in [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America") and one of the most populous cities in the world. As the most influential Brazilian city on the global stage, São Paulo is currently classified as an [alpha global city](/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network#Alpha "Globalization and World Cities Research Network").[[56]](#cite_note-GaWC-56) The metropolis has [one of the largest GDP in the world](/wiki/List_of_cities_by_GDP "List of cities by GDP"), representing, alone, 11% of all [Brazilian GDP](/wiki/Economy_of_Brazil "Economy of Brazil"),[[23]](#cite_note-Brazilian_GDP-23) and is also responsible for one third of the Brazilian scientific production.[[57]](#cite_note-Unicamp_2005-57)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Sao_Paulo_Brazil_Skyline_Aerial%2C_December_2024.jpg/960px-Sao_Paulo_Brazil_Skyline_Aerial%2C_December_2024.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sao_Paulo_Brazil_Skyline_Aerial,_December_2024.jpg "Skyline of São Paulo in 2024 from Aclimação. In the background, the buildings of Avenida Paulista, as well as Pico do Jaraguá to the right of the photograph.")

[Skyline of São Paulo](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "List of tallest buildings in São Paulo") in 2024 from [Aclimação](/wiki/Aclima%C3%A7%C3%A3o "Aclimação"). In the background, the buildings of [Avenida Paulista](/wiki/Avenida_Paulista "Avenida Paulista"), as well as [Pico do Jaraguá](/wiki/Pico_do_Jaragu%C3%A1 "Pico do Jaraguá") to the right of the photograph.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A3o_Paulo&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/17-08-2008_003Pico_do_Jaragu%C3%A1_in_S%C3%A3o_Paulo%28By_Felipe_Mostarda%29.JPG/250px-17-08-2008_003Pico_do_Jaragu%C3%A1_in_S%C3%A3o_Paulo%28By_Felipe_Mostarda%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:17-08-2008_003Pico_do_Jaragu%C3%A1_in_S%C3%A3o_Paulo(By_Felipe_Mostarda).JPG)

[Jaraguá Peak](/wiki/Pico_do_Jaragu%C3%A1 "Pico do Jaraguá") is the highest point in the city, at 1,135 meters (3,724 ft).[[58]](#cite_note-58)

São Paulo is the capital of the most populous state in Brazil, [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_(state) "São Paulo (state)"), located at [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude") 23°33'01'' south and [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude") 46°38'02'' west. The total area of the municipality is 1,521.11 square kilometres (587.30 sq mi), according to the [Brazilian Institute of Geography and Statistics](/wiki/Brazilian_Institute_of_Geography_and_Statistics "Brazilian Institute of Geography and Statistics") (IBGE), being the ninth largest in the state in terms of territorial extension.[[59]](#cite_note-IBGE_SãoPaulo-59) Of the entire area of the municipality, 949,611 square kilometres (366,647 sq mi) are urban areas (2015), being the largest urban area in the country.[[60]](#cite_note-EMBRAPA-2015-60)

The city is on a [plateau](/wiki/Plateau "Plateau") placed beyond the [Serra do Mar](/wiki/Serra_do_Mar "Serra do Mar") (Portuguese for "Sea Range" or "Coastal Range"), itself a component of the vast region known as the [Brazilian Highlands](/wiki/Brazilian_Highlands "Brazilian Highlands"), with an average elevation of around 799 meters (2,621 ft) [above sea level](/wiki/Above_mean_sea_level "Above mean sea level"), although being at a distance of only about 70 kilometers (43 mi) from [the Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"). The distance is covered by two highways, the [Anchieta](/wiki/Rodovia_Anchieta "Rodovia Anchieta") and the [Imigrantes](/wiki/Rodovia_dos_Imigrantes "Rodovia dos Imigrantes"), (see "[Transportation](#Transportation)" below) that roll down the range, leading to the port city of [Santos](/wiki/Santos,_Brazil "Santos, Brazil") and the beach resort of [Guarujá](/wiki/Guaruj%C3%A1 "Guarujá"). Rolling terrain prevails within the urbanized areas of São Paulo except in its northern area, where the [Serra da Cantareira](/wiki/Serra_da_Cantareira "Serra da Cantareira") Range reaches a higher elevation and a sizable remnant of the [Atlantic Rain Forest](/wiki/Atlantic_Forest "Atlantic Forest"). The region is seismically stable and no significant [activity](/wiki/Earthquake "Earthquake") has ever been recorded.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

### Hydrography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A3o_Paulo&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Hydrography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Marginal_tiet%C3%AA.jpg/250px-Marginal_tiet%C3%AA.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marginal_tiet%C3%AA.jpg)

[Tietê River](/wiki/Tiet%C3%AA_River "Tietê River"), with the [Marginal Tietê](/wiki/Marginal_Tiet%C3%AA "Marginal Tietê") highway

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Billings_2008051703.jpg/250px-Billings_2008051703.jpg)](/wiki/File:Billings_2008051703.jpg)

[Billings Reservoir](/wiki/Billings_Reservoir "Billings Reservoir")

See also: [Water management in the Metropolitan Region of São Paulo](/wiki/Water_management_in_the_Metropolitan_Region_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Water management in the Metropolitan Region of São Paulo")

The [Tietê River](/wiki/Tiet%C3%AA_River "Tietê River") and its [tributary](/wiki/Tributary "Tributary"), the [Pinheiros River](/wiki/Pinheiros_River "Pinheiros River"), were once important sources of [fresh water](/wiki/Freshwater "Freshwater") and leisure for São Paulo. However, heavy industrial [effluents](/wiki/Effluents "Effluents") and [wastewater](/wiki/Wastewater "Wastewater") discharges in the later 20th century caused the rivers to become [heavily polluted](/wiki/Water_pollution "Water pollution"). A substantial clean-up program for both rivers is underway.[[62]](#cite_note-veja.abril.com.br-62)[[63]](#cite_note-www1.folha.uol.com.br-63) Neither river is navigable in the stretch that flows through the city, although [water transportation](/wiki/Water_transportation "Water transportation") becomes increasingly important on the Tietê river further downstream (near [river Paraná](/wiki/Paran%C3%A1_River "Paraná River")), as the river is part of the [River Plate](/wiki/R%C3%ADo_de_la_Plata "Río de la Plata") basin.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

No large natural lakes exist in the region, but the [Billings](/wiki/Billings_Reservoir "Billings Reservoir") and [Guarapiranga](/wiki/Guarapiranga "Guarapiranga") reservoirs in the city's southern outskirts are used for [power generation](/wiki/Electricity_generation "Electricity generation"), water storage and leisure activities, such as sailing. The original [flora](/wiki/Flora "Flora") consisted mainly of [broadleaf](/wiki/Flowering_plant "Flowering plant") [evergreens](/wiki/Evergreen "Evergreen"). [Non-native species](/wiki/Introduced_species "Introduced species") are common, as the mild climate and abundant rainfall permit a multitude of tropical, subtropical and temperate plants to be cultivated, especially the ubiquitous [eucalyptus](/wiki/Eucalyptus "Eucalyptus").[[65]](#cite_note-65)

The north of the municipality contains part of the 7,917 hectares (19,560 acres) [Cantareira State Park](/wiki/Cantareira_State_Park "Cantareira State Park"), created in 1962, which protects a large part of the metropolitan São Paulo water supply.[[66]](#cite_note-66) In 2015, São Paulo experienced a [major drought](/wiki/2015_Brazilian_drought "2015 Brazilian drought"), which led several cities in the state to start a rationing system.[[67]](#cite_note-nyt2.2015-67)

### Parks and biodiversity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A3o_Paulo&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Parks and biodiversity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Parque_Estadual_da_Cantareira_-_N%C3%BAcleo_%C3%81guas_Claras_-_Pedra_Grande-.JPG/250px-Parque_Estadual_da_Cantareira_-_N%C3%BAcleo_%C3%81guas_Claras_-_Pedra_Grande-.JPG)](/wiki/File:Parque_Estadual_da_Cantareira_-_N%C3%BAcleo_%C3%81guas_Claras_-_Pedra_Grande-.JPG)

[Cantareira State Park](/wiki/Cantareira_State_Park "Cantareira State Park"), part of the [São Paulo Green Belt Biosphere Reserve](/wiki/Green_belt#Brazil "Green belt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Imagens_da_Cidade_de_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_e_Zool%C3%B3gico_da_Capital_Paulista._%2846756953414%29.jpg/250px-Imagens_da_Cidade_de_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_e_Zool%C3%B3gico_da_Capital_Paulista._%2846756953414%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Imagens_da_Cidade_de_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_e_Zool%C3%B3gico_da_Capital_Paulista._(46756953414).jpg)

[São Paulo Zoo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Zoo "São Paulo Zoo") in the [Fontes do Ipiranga Biological Reserve](/wiki/Fontes_do_Ipiranga_Biological_Reserve "Fontes do Ipiranga Biological Reserve")

São Paulo is located in an [ecotone](/wiki/Ecotone "Ecotone") area between 3 [biomes](/wiki/Biome "Biome"): [mixed ombrophilous forest](/wiki/Araucaria_moist_forests "Araucaria moist forests"), dense ombrophilous forest and [cerrado](/wiki/Cerrado "Cerrado"); the latter had some plant species native to the [pampas](/wiki/Pampas "Pampas") in the city. There were several species typical of both biomes, among them we can mention: [araucarias](/wiki/Araucaria "Araucaria"), [pitangueiras](/wiki/Eugenia_uniflora "Eugenia uniflora"), [cambucís](/wiki/Campomanesia_phaea "Campomanesia phaea"), [ipês](/wiki/Handroanthus "Handroanthus"), [jabuticabeiras](/wiki/Campomanesia_phaea "Campomanesia phaea"), [queen palms](/wiki/Syagrus_romanzoffiana "Syagrus romanzoffiana"), [muricís-do-campo](/wiki/Byrsonima_basiloba "Byrsonima basiloba"), etc.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

In 2010, São Paulo had 62 municipal and state parks,[[69]](#cite_note-69) such as the [Cantareira State Park](/wiki/Cantareira_State_Park "Cantareira State Park"), part of the [São Paulo Green Belt Biosphere Reserve](/wiki/Green_belt#Brazil "Green belt") and home to one of the largest [urban forests](/wiki/Urban_forest "Urban forest") on the planet with 7,900 hectares (20,000 acres) of extension,[[70]](#cite_note-70) the Fontes do Ipiranga State Park, the [Ibirapuera Park](/wiki/Ibirapuera_Park "Ibirapuera Park"), the Tietê Ecological Park, the Capivari-Monos Environmental Protection Area, the [Serra do Mar State Park](/wiki/Serra_do_Mar_State_Park "Serra do Mar State Park"), [Villa-Lobos State Park](/wiki/Villa-Lobos_State_Park "Villa-Lobos State Park"), [People's Park](/wiki/People%27s_Park_(S%C3%A3o_Paulo) "People's Park (São Paulo)"), and the [Jaraguá State Park](/wiki/Jaragu%C3%A1_State_Park "Jaraguá State Park"), listed as a [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") in 1994.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

In 2009, São Paulo had 2,300 hectares (5,700 acres) of green area, less than 1.5% of the city's area[[72]](#cite_note-72) and below the 12 square metres (130 sq ft) per inhabitant recommended by the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") (WHO).[[73]](#cite_note-73) About 21% of the municipality's area is covered by green areas, including ecological reserves (2010 data).[[74]](#cite_note-74)[[75]](#cite_note-75)

In the municipality it is possible to observe forest birds that usually appear in the spring, due to the belt of native forest that still surrounds the metropolitan region. Species such as the [rufous-bellied thrush](/wiki/Rufous-bellied_thrush "Rufous-bellied thrush"), [golden-chevroned tanager](/wiki/Golden-chevroned_tanager "Golden-chevroned tanager"), [great kiskadee](/wiki/Great_kiskadee "Great kiskadee") and hummingbird are the most common. Despite the intense pollution, the main rivers of the city, the Tietê and the Pinheiros, shelter several species of animals such as [capybaras](/wiki/Capybara "Capybara"), [hawks](/wiki/Hawk "Hawk"), [southern lapwings](/wiki/Southern_lapwing "Southern lapwing"), [herons](/wiki/Heron "Heron") and [nutrias](/wiki/Nutria "Nutria"). Other species found in the municipality are the [gray brocket](/wiki/Gray_brocket "Gray brocket"), [howler monkey](/wiki/Howler_monkey "Howler monkey"), [green-billed toucan](/wiki/Green-billed_toucan "Green-billed toucan") and the [Amazonian umbrellabird](/wiki/Amazonian_umbrellabird "Amazonian umbrellabird").[[76]](#cite_note-76)

### Environment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A3o_Paulo&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Environment")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/At_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_2018_331.jpg/250px-At_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_2018_331.jpg)](/wiki/File:At_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_2018_331.jpg)

[Tietê River](/wiki/Tiet%C3%AA_River "Tietê River"), one of the most polluted in the city

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Pollution_over_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_%28Jardins%29.jpg/250px-Pollution_over_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_%28Jardins%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pollution_over_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_(Jardins).jpg)

[Smog](/wiki/Smog "Smog") seen on the horizon of the Ibirapuera region

[Air pollution](/wiki/Air_pollution "Air pollution") in some districts of the city exceeds local standards, mainly due to car traffic.[[77]](#cite_note-Ribeiro-77) The [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") (WHO) sets a limit of 20 micrograms of particulate matter per cubic meter of air as a safe annual average. In an assessment carried out by the WHO among over a thousand cities around the world in 2011, the city of São Paulo was ranked 268th among the most polluted, with an average rate of 38 micrograms per cubic meter, a rate well above the limit imposed by the organization, but lower than in other Brazilian cities, such as [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro") (64 micrograms per cubic meter).[[78]](#cite_note-78) A 2013 study found that air pollution in the city causes more deaths than [traffic accidents](/wiki/Traffic_accidents "Traffic accidents").[[79]](#cite_note-79)[[80]](#cite_note-80)

The stretch of the [Tietê River](/wiki/Tiet%C3%AA_River "Tietê River") that runs through the city is the most polluted river in Brazil.[[81]](#cite_note-81) In 1992, the Tietê Project began, with the aim to clean up the river by 2005. 8.8 billion reais was spent on the failed project.[[82]](#cite_note-BBC-82) In 2019, the Novo Rio Pinheiros Project began, under the administration of [João Doria](/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_Doria "João Doria"), with the aim to reduce sewage discharged into the Tietê's tributary, the [Pinheiros River](/wiki/Pinheiros_River "Pinheiros River").[[83]](#cite_note-83)[[84]](#cite_note-84)

The problem of balanced water supply for the city – and for the metropolis, in general – is also a worrying issue: São Paulo has few sources of water in its own perimeter, having to seek it in distant hydrographic basins. The problem of water pollution is also aggravated by the irregular occupation of watershed areas, caused by urban expansion, driven by the difficulty of access to land and housing in central areas by the low-income population[[85]](#cite_note-85) and associated with real estate speculation and precariousness in new subdivisions. With this, there is also an overvaluation of individual transport over public transport – leading to the current rate of more than one vehicle for every two inhabitants and aggravating the problem of environmental pollution.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A3o_Paulo&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Climate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Tempestade_em_SP.jpg/250px-Tempestade_em_SP.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tempestade_em_SP.jpg)

Heavy rain and lightning in São Paulo, which has one of the largest number of [lightning](/wiki/Lightning "Lightning") incidents amongst Brazilian state capitals[[87]](#cite_note-87)

São Paulo features a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *Cwa*, [Trewartha](/wiki/Trewartha_climate_classification "Trewartha climate classification"): *Cwal*).[[88]](#cite_note-Climate-88) In summer (January through March), the mean low temperature is about 19 °C (66 °F) and the mean high temperatures is near 28 °C (82 °F). In winter, temperatures tend to range between 12 and 22 °C (54 and 72 °F). The record high temperature was 38.4 °C (101.1 °F) on 17 October 2014[[89]](#cite_note-bdmep.inmet.gov.br-89) and the lowest −3.2 °C (26.2 °F) on 25 June 1918.[[90]](#cite_note-90)[[91]](#cite_note-91) The highest accumulated rainfall in 24 hours was 151.8 mm (5.98 in) on December 21, 1988, followed by 144.1 mm (5.67 in) on January 25, 2025, and 140.4 mm (5.53 in) on May 25, 2005.[[89]](#cite_note-bdmep.inmet.gov.br-89)

The [Tropic of Capricorn](/wiki/Tropic_of_Capricorn "Tropic of Capricorn"), at about 23°27' S, passes through north of São Paulo and roughly marks the boundary between the tropical and [temperate](/wiki/Temperateness "Temperateness") areas of South America. Because of its elevation, however, São Paulo experiences a more temperate climate.[[92]](#cite_note-92) The summer is hot and rainy. Autumn and spring are transitional seasons. Winter is mild, but still the coldest season, with cloudiness around town and frequent polar air masses. [Frosts](/wiki/Frost "Frost") occur sporadically in regions further away from the center, in some winters throughout the city.[[93]](#cite_note-93)

Rainfall is abundant, annually averaging 1,658 millimeters (65.3 in). It is especially common in the warmer months averaging 292 millimeters (11.5 in) in January and decreases in winter, averaging 32 millimeters (1.3 in).[[94]](#cite_note-INMETprecip-94) Neither São Paulo nor the nearby coast has ever been hit by a [tropical cyclone](/wiki/Tropical_cyclone "Tropical cyclone") and [tornadic](/wiki/Tornado "Tornado") activity is uncommon. During late winter, especially August, the city experiences the phenomenon known as *"veranico"* or *"verãozinho"* ("little summer"), which consists of hot and dry weather, sometimes reaching temperatures well above 28 °C (82 °F). On the other hand, relatively cool days during summer are fairly common when persistent winds blow from the ocean. On such occasions daily high temperatures may not surpass 20 °C (68 °F), accompanied by lows often below 15 °C (59 °F), however, spring and summer can be extremely hot when a heat wave hits the city followed by temperatures around 34 °C (93 °F), but in places with greater skyscraper density and less tree cover, the temperature can feel like 39 °C (102 °F), as on [Paulista Avenue](/wiki/Paulista_Avenue "Paulista Avenue") for example. In the summer of 2014, São Paulo was affected by a heat wave that lasted for almost 4 weeks with highs above 30 °C (86 °F), peaking on 36 °C (97 °F). Secondary to [deforestation](/wiki/Deforestation "Deforestation"), [groundwater pollution](/wiki/Groundwater_pollution "Groundwater pollution"), and [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change"), São Paulo is increasingly susceptible to [drought](/wiki/Drought "Drought") and [water shortages](/wiki/Water_shortage "Water shortage").[[95]](#cite_note-95)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A3o_Paulo&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Demographics")]

See also: [Brazilians](/wiki/Brazilians "Brazilians") and [Demographics of Brazil](/wiki/Demographics_of_Brazil "Demographics of Brazil")

São Paulo's population has grown rapidly. By 1960 it had surpassed that of [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), making it Brazil's most populous city. By this time, the urbanized area of São Paulo had extended beyond the boundaries of the municipality proper into neighboring municipalities, making it a metropolitan area with a population of 4.6 million. Population growth has continued since 1960, although the rate of growth has slowed.[[115]](#cite_note-115)

In 2013, São Paulo was the most populous city in Brazil and in South America.[[116]](#cite_note-116) According to the [2010 IBGE Census](/wiki/Brazilian_Institute_of_Geography_and_Statistics "Brazilian Institute of Geography and Statistics"), there were 11,244,369 people residing in the city of São Paulo.[[117]](#cite_note-117) Portuguese remains the most widely spoken language and São Paulo is the largest city in the [Portuguese speaking world](/wiki/Portuguese_speaking_world "Portuguese speaking world").[[118]](#cite_note-118)

In 2010, the city had 2,146,077 opposite-sex couples and 7,532 [same-sex couples](/wiki/Same-sex_couple "Same-sex couple"). The population of São Paulo was 52.6% female and 47.4% male.[[119]](#cite_note-censo2010.ibge.gov.br-119) The [2022 census](/wiki/2022_Brazilian_census "2022 Brazilian census") found 6,214,422 [White](/wiki/White_Brazilian "White Brazilian") people (54.3%), 3,820,326 [Pardo](/wiki/Pardo_Brazilians "Pardo Brazilians") ([multiracial](/wiki/Multiracial#Brazil "Multiracial")) people (33.4%), 1,160,073 [Black](/wiki/Afro-Brazilian "Afro-Brazilian") people (10.1%), 238,603 [Asian](/wiki/Asian_Brazilian "Asian Brazilian") people (2.1%) and 17,727 [Amerindian](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Brazil "Indigenous peoples in Brazil") people (0.2%).[[120]](#cite_note-120)

### Immigration and migration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A3o_Paulo&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Immigration and migration")]

Main article: [Immigration to Brazil](/wiki/Immigration_to_Brazil "Immigration to Brazil")

Race and ethnicity in São Paulo

|  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Ethnicity | |  | Percentage | |
| [White](/wiki/White_Brazilian "White Brazilian") | |  | 54.3% | |
| [Mixed](/wiki/Pardo "Pardo") | |  | 33.4% | |
| [Black](/wiki/Black_Brazilian "Black Brazilian") | |  | 10.1% | |
| [Asian](/wiki/Asian_Brazilian "Asian Brazilian") | |  | 2.1% | |
| [Amerindian](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Brazil "Indigenous peoples in Brazil") | |  | 0.2% | |

São Paulo is considered the most [multicultural](/wiki/Multicultural "Multicultural") city in Brazil. From 1870 to 2010, approximately 2.3 million [immigrants](/wiki/Immigrants "Immigrants") arrived in the state, from all parts of the world. The [Italian community](/wiki/Italian_Brazilians "Italian Brazilians") is one of the strongest, with a presence throughout the city. Of the 12 million inhabitants of São Paulo, 50% (5 million people) have full or partial Italian ancestry. São Paulo has more [descendants of Italians](/wiki/Italian_Brazilians "Italian Brazilians") than any Italian city (the largest city of [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") is [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), with 2.8 million inhabitants).[[121]](#cite_note-121)

The main groups, considering all the metropolitan area, are: 6 million people of [Portuguese descent](/wiki/Portuguese_Brazilian "Portuguese Brazilian"),[[122]](#cite_note-122) 5 million people of [Italian descent](/wiki/Italian_Brazilian "Italian Brazilian"),[[123]](#cite_note-123) 1.7 million people of [African descent](/wiki/African_Brazilian "African Brazilian"),[[124]](#cite_note-124) 1 million people of [Arab descent](/wiki/Arab_Brazilian "Arab Brazilian"),[[125]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-125) 665,000 people of [Japanese descent](/wiki/Japanese_Brazilian "Japanese Brazilian"),[[125]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-125) 400,000 people of [German descent](/wiki/German_Brazilian "German Brazilian"),[[125]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-125) 250,000 people of [French descent](/wiki/French_Brazilian "French Brazilian"),[[125]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-125) 150,000 people of [Greek descent](/wiki/Greek_Brazilian "Greek Brazilian"),[[125]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-125) 120,000 people of [Chinese descent](/wiki/Chinese_Brazilian "Chinese Brazilian"),[[125]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-125) 120,000–300,000 [Bolivian immigrants](/wiki/Bolivians_in_Brazil "Bolivians in Brazil"),[[126]](#cite_note-126) 50,000 people of [Korean descent](/wiki/Korean_Brazilian "Korean Brazilian"),[[127]](#cite_note-127) and 80,000 [Jews](/wiki/Jews_in_Brazil "Jews in Brazil").[[128]](#cite_note-128)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Italians_Sao_Paulo_-_original.jpg/250px-Italians_Sao_Paulo_-_original.jpg)](/wiki/File:Italians_Sao_Paulo_-_original.jpg)

[Italian immigrants](/wiki/Italian_Brazilian "Italian Brazilian") in the [Immigrant Inn](/wiki/Immigrant_Inn "Immigrant Inn"), c. 1890

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Liberdade_of_Sao_Paulo.jpg/250px-Liberdade_of_Sao_Paulo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Liberdade_of_Sao_Paulo.jpg)

The [Liberdade](/wiki/Liberdade_(district_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo) "Liberdade (district of São Paulo)") district is a [Japantown](/wiki/Japantown "Japantown") of São Paulo.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Casa_de_Portugal_S%C3%A3o_Paulo.JPG/250px-Casa_de_Portugal_S%C3%A3o_Paulo.JPG)](/wiki/File:Casa_de_Portugal_S%C3%A3o_Paulo.JPG)

[Casa de Portugal in São Paulo](/wiki/Casa_de_Portugal_in_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Casa de Portugal in São Paulo") headquarters

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Memorial_da_Imigra%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Judaica_06.jpg/250px-Memorial_da_Imigra%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Judaica_06.jpg)](/wiki/File:Memorial_da_Imigra%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Judaica_06.jpg)

Photos of [Jewish immigrants](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Brazil "History of the Jews in Brazil") at the Jewish Immigration Memorial in [Bom Retiro](/wiki/Bom_Retiro_(district_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo) "Bom Retiro (district of São Paulo)")

Even today, Italians are grouped in neighborhoods like [Bixiga](/wiki/Bixiga "Bixiga"), [Brás](/wiki/Br%C3%A1s "Brás"), and [Mooca](/wiki/Mooca_(district_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo) "Mooca (district of São Paulo)") to promote celebrations and festivals. In the early twentieth century, Italian and its dialects were spoken almost as much as [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") in the city, which influenced the formation of the São Paulo dialect of today. Six thousand pizzerias are producing about a million pizzas a day. Brazil has the largest [Italian population](/wiki/Italians "Italians") outside [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), with São Paulo being the most populous city with Italian ancestry in the world.[[129]](#cite_note-129)

The [Portuguese community](/wiki/Portuguese_Brazilians "Portuguese Brazilians") not surprisingly is the largest, consisting of 6 million inhabitants. From the beginning, the Portuguese formed the root and foundation of Brazil, as they installed the Portuguese language, culture, the Catholic religion, laws, government bodies, courts, educational institutions, etc, in Brazil. Paulistanos have considerable origin in [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal"). The [Jewish colony](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Brazil "History of the Jews in Brazil") is more than 80,000 people in São Paulo and is concentrated mainly in [Higienópolis](/wiki/Higien%C3%B3polis,_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Higienópolis, São Paulo") and [Bom Retiro](/wiki/Bom_Retiro_(district_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo) "Bom Retiro (district of São Paulo)").[[130]](#cite_note-forbesimi-130)

From the nineteenth century through the first half of the twentieth century, São Paulo also received [German immigrants](/wiki/German_Brazilians "German Brazilians") (in the current neighborhood of [Santo Amaro](/wiki/Santo_Amaro,_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Santo Amaro, São Paulo")), [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_people "Spanish people") and [Lithuanian](/wiki/Lithuanian_people "Lithuanian people") (in the neighborhood [Vila Zelina](/wiki/Vila_Zelina "Vila Zelina")).[[130]](#cite_note-forbesimi-130)

> "A French observer, travelling to São Paulo at the time, noted that there was a division of the capitalist class, by nationality (...) Germans, French and Italians shared the dry goods sector with Brazilians. Foodstuffs was generally the province of either Portuguese or Brazilians, except for bakery and pastry which was the domain of the French and Germans. Shoes and tinware were mostly controlled by Italians. However, the larger metallurgical plants were in the hands of the English and the Americans. (...) Italians outnumbered Brazilians two to one in São Paulo."

— [[131]](#cite_note-131)

Until 1920, 1,078,437 [Italians](/wiki/Italians "Italians") entered in the [State of São Paulo](/wiki/State_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "State of São Paulo"). Between 1888 and 1919, 38% of the immigrants were Italians, 10.2% were [Spaniards](/wiki/Spaniards "Spaniards") and 40% were [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_people "Portuguese people").[[132]](#cite_note-100_anos_de_imigração-132) In 1920, nearly 80% of São Paulo city's population was composed of immigrants and their descendants and Italians made up over half of its male population.[[132]](#cite_note-100_anos_de_imigração-132)
São Paulo also is home of the largest [Japanese community](/wiki/Japanese_diaspora "Japanese diaspora") outside [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan").[[133]](#cite_note-133) In 1958 the census counted 120,000 Japanese in the city and by 1987, there were 326,000 with another 170,000 in the surrounding areas within São Paulo state.[[134]](#cite_note-Lesserp3-134) As of 2007, the [Paulistano Japanese population](/wiki/Japanese_community_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Japanese community of São Paulo") outnumbered their fellow diaspora in [the entirety of Peru](/wiki/Japanese_Peruvian "Japanese Peruvian"), and in all individual American cities.[[134]](#cite_note-Lesserp3-134)

Research conducted by the [University of São Paulo](/wiki/University_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "University of São Paulo") (USP) shows the city's high ethnic diversity: when asked if they are "descendants of foreign immigrants", 81% of the students reported "yes". The main reported ancestries were: Italian (30.5%), Portuguese (35%), Spanish (14%), Japanese (8%), German (6%), *Brazilian* (4%), African (3%), Arab (2%) and Jewish (1%).[[135]](#cite_note-135)

The city once attracted numerous immigrants from all over Brazil and even from foreign countries, due to a strong economy and for being the hub of most Brazilian companies.[[136]](#cite_note-136) São Paulo is also receiving waves of immigration from [Haiti](/wiki/Haiti "Haiti") and from many countries of [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa") and the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean"). Those immigrants are mainly concentrated in [Praça da Sé](/wiki/Pra%C3%A7a_da_S%C3%A9 "Praça da Sé"), [Glicério](/wiki/Glic%C3%A9rio "Glicério") and [Vale do Anhangabaú](/wiki/Vale_do_Anhangaba%C3%BA "Vale do Anhangabaú") in the [Central Zone of São Paulo](/wiki/Central_Zone_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Central Zone of São Paulo").

Since the 19th century people began migrating from [northeastern Brazil](/wiki/Northeast_Region,_Brazil "Northeast Region, Brazil") into São Paulo. This migration grew enormously in the 1930s and remained huge in the next decades. The concentration of land, modernization in rural areas, changes in work relationships and cycles of droughts stimulated migration. The largest concentration of northeastern migrants was found in the area of Sé/Brás (districts of [Brás](/wiki/Br%C3%A1s "Brás"), [Bom Retiro](/wiki/Bom_Retiro,_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Bom Retiro, São Paulo"), [Cambuci](/wiki/Cambuci_(district_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo) "Cambuci (district of São Paulo)"), [Pari](/wiki/Pari_(district_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo) "Pari (district of São Paulo)") and [Sé](/wiki/S%C3%A9_(district_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo) "Sé (district of São Paulo)")). In this area they composed 41% of the population.[[137]](#cite_note-137)

### Metropolitan area

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A3o_Paulo&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Metropolitan area")]

Main articles: [Greater São Paulo](/wiki/Greater_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Greater São Paulo") and [São Paulo Macrometropolis](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Macrometropolis "São Paulo Macrometropolis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/ISS-67_City_lights_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo%2C_Brazil.jpg/250px-ISS-67_City_lights_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo%2C_Brazil.jpg)](/wiki/File:ISS-67_City_lights_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo,_Brazil.jpg)

Satellite view of [Greater São Paulo](/wiki/Greater_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Greater São Paulo") at night

The nonspecific term "Grande São Paulo" ("*[Greater São Paulo](/wiki/Greater_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Greater São Paulo")*") covers multiple definitions. The legally defined *Região Metropolitana de São Paulo* consists of 39 municipalities in total and a population of 21.1 million[[138]](#cite_note-bra-138) inhabitants (as of the 2014 National Census[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A3o_Paulo&action=edit)).

Because São Paulo has [urban sprawl](/wiki/Urban_sprawl "Urban sprawl"), it uses a different definition for its metropolitan area alternately called the Expanded Metropolitan Complex of São Paulo and the [São Paulo Macrometropolis](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Macrometropolis "São Paulo Macrometropolis"). Analogous to the [BosWash](/wiki/Northeast_megalopolis "Northeast megalopolis") definition, it is one of the largest urban agglomerations in the world, with 32 million inhabitants,[[139]](#cite_note-139) behind [Tokyo](/wiki/Greater_Tokyo_Area "Greater Tokyo Area"), which includes four contiguous legally defined metropolitan regions and three micro-regions.
