Like the cultural variety verifiable in São Paulo, there are several religious manifestations present in the city. Although it has developed on an eminently [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") social matrix, both due to colonization and immigration – and even today most of the people of São Paulo declare themselves Roman Catholic – it is possible to find in the city dozens of different Protestant denominations, as well as the practice of [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), [Spiritism](/wiki/Kardecist_spiritism "Kardecist spiritism"), among others. [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") and Eastern religions also have relevance among the beliefs most practiced by Paulistanos. It is estimated that there are more than one hundred thousand [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhist "Buddhist") and [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") followers each. Also considerable are [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism"), [Mormonism](/wiki/Mormonism "Mormonism") and [Afro-Brazilian religions](/wiki/Afro-Brazilian_religions "Afro-Brazilian religions").[[140]](#cite_note-Religion-140)

According to data from the [Brazilian Institute of Geography and Statistics](/wiki/Brazilian_Institute_of_Geography_and_Statistics "Brazilian Institute of Geography and Statistics") (IBGE), in 2010 the population of São Paulo was 6,549,775 [Roman Catholics](/wiki/Roman_Catholics "Roman Catholics") (58.2%), 2,887,810 [Protestants](/wiki/Protestants "Protestants") (22.1%), 531,822 [Spiritists](/wiki/Spiritists "Spiritists") (4.7%), 101,493 [Jehovah's Witnesses](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Jehovah's Witnesses") (0.9%), 75,075 [Buddhists](/wiki/Buddhists "Buddhists") (0.7%), 50,794 [Umbandists](/wiki/Umbanda "Umbanda") (0.5%), 43,610 [Jews](/wiki/Jews "Jews") (0.4%), 28,673 [Catholic Apostolic Brazilians](/wiki/Brazilian_Catholic_Apostolic_Church "Brazilian Catholic Apostolic Church") (0.3%), 25,583 eastern religious (0.2%), 18,058 [Candomblecists](/wiki/Candombl%C3%A9 "Candomblé") (0.2%), 17,321 [Mormons](/wiki/Mormons "Mormons") (0.2%), 14,894 Eastern Orthodox (0.1%), 9,119 [spiritualists](/wiki/Spiritualism_(movement) "Spiritualism (movement)") (0.1%), 8,277 [Muslims](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims") (0.1%), 7,139 [esoteric](/wiki/Esoteric "Esoteric") (0.1%), 1,829 practiced Indian traditions (<0.1%) and 1,008 were [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") (<0.1%). Others 1,056 008 had no religion (9.4%), 149,628 followed other Christian religiosities (1.3%), 55,978 had an undetermined religion or multiple belonging (0.5%), 14,127 did not know (0.1%) And 1,896 reported following other religiosities (<0.1%).[[140]](#cite_note-Religion-140)

The Catholic Church divides the territory of the municipality of São Paulo into four ecclesiastical circumscriptions: the [Archdiocese of São Paulo](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Archdiocese of São Paulo"), and the adjacent Diocese of Santo Amaro, the Diocese of São Miguel Paulista and the Diocese of Campo Limpo, the last three suffragans of the first. The archive of the archdiocese, called the Metropolitan Archival Dom Duarte Leopoldo e Silva, in the [Ipiranga](/wiki/Ipiranga_(district_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo) "Ipiranga (district of São Paulo)") neighborhood, holds one of the most important documentary heritage in Brazil. The archiepiscopal is the [Metropolitan Cathedral of São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Cathedral "São Paulo Cathedral") (known as Sé Cathedral), in [Praça da Sé](/wiki/Pra%C3%A7a_da_S%C3%A9 "Praça da Sé"), considered one of the five largest [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_Revival_architecture "Gothic Revival architecture") temples in the world. The Catholic Church recognizes as patron saints of the city [Saint Paul of Tarsus](/wiki/Paul_the_Apostle "Paul the Apostle") and [Our Lady of Penha of France](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_Pe%C3%B1afrancia "Our Lady of Peñafrancia").[[141]](#cite_note-141)[[142]](#cite_note-IBGE_2010-142)

The city has the most diverse Protestant or Reformed creeds, such as the Evangelical Community of Our Land, Maranatha Christian Church, [Lutheran Church](/wiki/Lutheran_Church "Lutheran Church"), [Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church "Presbyterian Church"), [Methodist Church](/wiki/Methodist_Church "Methodist Church"), [Anglican Episcopal Church](/wiki/Anglican_Episcopal_Church "Anglican Episcopal Church"), [Baptist churches](/wiki/Baptist_church "Baptist church"), [Assemblies of God in Brazil](/wiki/Assembleias_de_Deus "Assembleias de Deus") (the largest evangelical church in the country),[[143]](#cite_note-143)[[144]](#cite_note-144) The [Seventh-day Adventist Church](/wiki/Seventh-day_Adventist_Church "Seventh-day Adventist Church"), the World Church of God's Power, the Universal Church of the Kingdom of God, the [Christian Congregation in Brazil](/wiki/Christian_Congregation_in_Brazil "Christian Congregation in Brazil"),[[145]](#cite_note-145) among others, as well as Christians of various denominations.[[142]](#cite_note-IBGE_2010-142)

### Public security

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A3o_Paulo&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Public security")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Military_Police_Operations_Command_%28COPOM%29_of_the_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_State_Military_Police.jpg/250px-Military_Police_Operations_Command_%28COPOM%29_of_the_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_State_Military_Police.jpg)](/wiki/File:Military_Police_Operations_Command_(COPOM)_of_the_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_State_Military_Police.jpg)

[Tactical operations center](/wiki/Tactical_operations_center "Tactical operations center") of the [Military Police of São Paulo State](/wiki/Military_Police_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_State "Military Police of São Paulo State") in São Paulo

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Formacaodapmesp.jpg/250px-Formacaodapmesp.jpg)](/wiki/File:Formacaodapmesp.jpg)

Training of soldiers of the [Military Police of São Paulo State](/wiki/Military_Police_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_State "Military Police of São Paulo State") at the Military Police Academy of Barro Branco

In 2008, the city of São Paulo ranked 493rd on the list of the most violent cities in Brazil. Among the capitals, it was the fourth least violent, registering, in 2006, homicide rates higher only than those of [Boa Vista](/wiki/Boa_Vista,_Roraima "Boa Vista, Roraima"), [Palmas](/wiki/Palmas,_Tocantins "Palmas, Tocantins") and [Natal](/wiki/Natal,_Rio_Grande_do_Norte "Natal, Rio Grande do Norte").[[146]](#cite_note-146)[[147]](#cite_note-147) In November 2009, the Ministry of Justice and the Brazilian Public Security Forum released a survey that identified São Paulo as the safest Brazilian capital for young people.[[148]](#cite_note-148) Between 2000 and 2010, the city of São Paulo reduced its homicide rate by 78%.[[149]](#cite_note-149)

According to the 2011 Global Homicide Study, released by the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") (UN), in the period between 2004 and 2009 the homicide rate fell from 20.8 to 10.8 murders per hundred thousand inhabitants. In 2011, the UN pointed to São Paulo as an example of how large cities can reduce crime.[[150]](#cite_note-150) In a survey on the Adolescent Homicide Index (AHI) in 2010, the city of São Paulo was considered the least lethal for adolescents, among 283 municipalities surveyed, with more than 100,000 inhabitants.[[151]](#cite_note-151) According to data from the "Map of Violence 2011", published by the Sangari Institute and the Ministry of Justice, the city had the lowest homicide rate per hundred thousand inhabitants that year among all the state capitals in Brazil.[[152]](#cite_note-Mapa_da_violência-152)

Crime indicators, such as homicide, according to data from April 2017, showed a reduction in the capital of São Paulo, compared to 2016. In the same period, there was a 12.64% reduction in homicides, the number of robbery records fell by eleven to seven (34% reduction), and there was an 8.09% reduction in rape cases.[[153]](#cite_note-153) The 9th DP in the Carandiru neighborhood was considered, in March 2007, one of the five best police stations in the world and the best in [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America").[[154]](#cite_note-154)

Based on data from IBGE and the Ministry of Health, it is considered the 2nd safest capital[[155]](#cite_note-155) and the least lethal capital in the country, according to the 2023 Brazilian Public Security Yearbook.[[156]](#cite_note-156)

### Social challenges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A3o_Paulo&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Social challenges")]

Main article: [Social issues in Brazil](/wiki/Social_issues_in_Brazil "Social issues in Brazil")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Parais%C3%B3polis_I.jpg/250px-Parais%C3%B3polis_I.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parais%C3%B3polis_I.jpg)

[Paraisópolis](/wiki/Parais%C3%B3polis_(S%C3%A3o_Paulo) "Paraisópolis (São Paulo)"), the largest [slum](/wiki/Slum "Slum") (*[favela](/wiki/Favela "Favela")*) in the city, with residential buildings in the background

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Conjunto_habitacional_em_Heli%C3%B3polis.jpg/250px-Conjunto_habitacional_em_Heli%C3%B3polis.jpg)](/wiki/File:Conjunto_habitacional_em_Heli%C3%B3polis.jpg)

[Public housing](/wiki/Public_housing "Public housing") in [Heliópolis](/wiki/Heli%C3%B3polis "Heliópolis")

Since the beginning of the 20th century, São Paulo has been a major economic center in Latin America. During two [World Wars](/wiki/World_war "World war") and the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"), coffee exports (from other regions of the state) were critically affected. This led wealthy coffee farmers to invest in industrial activities that turned São Paulo into Brazil's largest industrial hub.

* [Crime rates](/wiki/Crime_statistics "Crime statistics") consistently decreased in the 21st century. The citywide homicide rate was 6.56 in 2019, less than a fourth of the 27.38 national rate.[[157]](#cite_note-157)
* Air quality[[77]](#cite_note-Ribeiro-77) has steadily increased during the modern era.
* The two major rivers crossing the city, [Tietê](/wiki/Tiet%C3%AA_River "Tietê River") and [Pinheiros](/wiki/Pinheiros_River "Pinheiros River"), are highly polluted. A major project to clean up these rivers is underway.[[62]](#cite_note-veja.abril.com.br-62)[[63]](#cite_note-www1.folha.uol.com.br-63)
* The [Clean City Law](/wiki/Cidade_Limpa "Cidade Limpa") or anti[billboard](/wiki/Billboard "Billboard"), approved in 2007, focused on two main targets: anti-publicity and anti-commerce. Advertisers estimate that they removed 15,000 billboards and that more than 1,600 [signs](/wiki/Signage "Signage") and 1,300 towering metal panels were dismantled by authorities.[[158]](#cite_note-158)
* São Paulo metropolitan region, adopted vehicle restrictions from 1996 to 1998 to reduce air pollution during wintertime. Since 1997, a similar project was implemented throughout the year in the central area of São Paulo to improve traffic.[[159]](#cite_note-159)
* There were more than 30,000 homeless people in 2021 according to official data. It increased by 31% in two years, and doubled in 20 years.[[160]](#cite_note-160)

### Languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A3o_Paulo&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Languages")]

Main article: [Languages of Brazil](/wiki/Languages_of_Brazil "Languages of Brazil")

The primary language is [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"). The general language from São Paulo General, or [Tupi Austral](/wiki/Tupi_language "Tupi language") (Southern Tupi), was the Tupi-based trade language of what is now São Vicente, São Paulo, and the upper Tietê River. In the 17th century it was widely spoken in São Paulo and spread to neighboring regions while in Brazil. From 1750 on, following orders from [Marquess of Pombal](/wiki/Marquess_of_Pombal "Marquess of Pombal"), Portuguese language was introduced through immigration and consequently taught to children in schools. The original [Tupi Austral](/wiki/Tupi_Austral "Tupi Austral") language subsequently lost ground to [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"), and eventually became extinct. Due to the large influx of [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_Brazilians "Japanese Brazilians"), [German](/wiki/German_Brazilians "German Brazilians"), [Spanish](/wiki/Brazilians_of_Spanish_descent "Brazilians of Spanish descent"), [Italian](/wiki/Italian_Brazilian "Italian Brazilian") and [Arab](/wiki/Arab_Brazilian "Arab Brazilian") immigrants etc., the Portuguese idiom spoken in the metropolitan area of São Paulo reflects influences from those languages.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Interior_do_Museu_da_L%C3%ADngua_Portuguesa%2C_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_%28SP%29.jpg/250px-Interior_do_Museu_da_L%C3%ADngua_Portuguesa%2C_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_%28SP%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Interior_do_Museu_da_L%C3%ADngua_Portuguesa,_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_(SP).jpg)

[Museum of the Portuguese Language](/wiki/Museum_of_the_Portuguese_Language "Museum of the Portuguese Language") interior

The Italian influence in São Paulo accents is evident in the Italian neighborhoods such as Bela Vista, [Mooca](/wiki/Mooca_(district_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo) "Mooca (district of São Paulo)"), [Brás](/wiki/Br%C3%A1s "Brás") and Lapa. Italian mingled with Portuguese and as an old influence, was assimilated or disappeared into spoken language. The local accent with Italian influences became notorious through the songs of [Adoniran Barbosa](/wiki/Adoniran_Barbosa "Adoniran Barbosa"), a Brazilian [samba](/wiki/Samba "Samba") singer born to Italian parents who used to sing using the local accent.[[161]](#cite_note-161)

Other languages spoken in the city are mainly among the Asian community: São Paulo is home to the largest Japanese population outside Japan. Although today most Japanese-Brazilians speak only Portuguese, some of them are still fluent in Japanese. Some people of Chinese and Korean descent are still able to speak their ancestral languages.[[162]](#cite_note-162) In some areas it is still possible to find descendants of immigrants who speak German[[163]](#cite_note-163) (especially in the area of Brooklin paulista) and Lithuanian or Russian or East European languages (especially in the area of Vila Zelina).[[164]](#cite_note-164)[[165]](#cite_note-BBC_Brasil-165) In the west zone of São Paulo, specially at Vila Anastácio and Lapa region, there is a [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_people "Hungarian people") colony, with three churches (Calvinist, Baptist and Catholic), so on Sundays it is possible to see Hungarians talking to each other on sidewalks.

### Sexual diversity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A3o_Paulo&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Sexual diversity")]

Main articles: [LGBT rights in Brazil](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_Brazil "LGBT rights in Brazil"), [LGBT people in Brazil](/wiki/LGBT_people_in_Brazil "LGBT people in Brazil"), [LGBT history in Brazil](/wiki/LGBT_history_in_Brazil "LGBT history in Brazil"), and [Same-sex marriage in Brazil](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_Brazil "Same-sex marriage in Brazil")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_LGBT_Pride_Parade_2014_%2814108541924%29.jpg/250px-S%C3%A3o_Paulo_LGBT_Pride_Parade_2014_%2814108541924%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:S%C3%A3o_Paulo_LGBT_Pride_Parade_2014_(14108541924).jpg)

The 18th edition of the [São Paulo Gay Pride Parade](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Gay_Pride_Parade "São Paulo Gay Pride Parade") in 2014

The [Greater São Paulo](/wiki/Greater_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Greater São Paulo") is home to a prominent self-identifying [gay](/wiki/Gay "Gay"), [bisexual](/wiki/Bisexual "Bisexual") and [transgender](/wiki/Transgender "Transgender") community, with 9.6% of the male population and 7% of the female population declaring themselves to be non-heterosexual.[[166]](#cite_note-166) Same-sex [civil unions](/wiki/Civil_union "Civil union") have been legal in the whole country since 5 May 2011, while [same-sex marriage in São Paulo](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_Brazil "Same-sex marriage in Brazil") was legalized on 18 December 2012. Since 1997, the city has hosted the annual [São Paulo Gay Pride Parade](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Gay_Pride_Parade "São Paulo Gay Pride Parade"), considered the biggest [pride parade](/wiki/Pride_parade "Pride parade") in the world by the [Guinness Book of World Records](/wiki/Guinness_Book_of_World_Records "Guinness Book of World Records") with over 5 million participants, and typically rivalling the [New York City Pride March](/wiki/New_York_City_Pride_March "New York City Pride March") for the record.[[40]](#cite_note-NYCWorld'sLargestPrideParade-40)

Strongly supported by the State and the City of São Paulo government authorities, in 2010, the city hall of São Paulo invested R$1 million reais in the parade and provided a solid security plan, with approximately 2,000 policemen, two mobile police stations for immediate reporting of occurrences, 30 equipped ambulances, 55 nurses, 46 medical physicians, three hospital camps with 80 beds. The parade, considered the city's second largest event after the [Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One"), begins at the [São Paulo Museum of Art](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Museum_of_Art "São Paulo Museum of Art"), crosses [Paulista Avenue](/wiki/Paulista_Avenue "Paulista Avenue"), and follows Consolação Street to [Praça Roosevelt](/wiki/Pra%C3%A7a_Roosevelt "Praça Roosevelt") in [Downtown São Paulo](/wiki/Downtown_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Downtown São Paulo"). According to the LGBT app [Grindr](/wiki/Grindr "Grindr"), the gay parade of the city was elected the best in the world.[[167]](#cite_note-167)

### Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A3o_Paulo&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Education")]

Main articles: [Education in Brazil](/wiki/Education_in_Brazil "Education in Brazil") and [Brazilian science and technology](/wiki/Brazilian_science_and_technology "Brazilian science and technology")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/University_sao_paulo.jpg/250px-University_sao_paulo.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_sao_paulo.jpg)

[University of São Paulo](/wiki/University_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "University of São Paulo")

São Paulo has public and private primary and secondary schools and vocational-[technical schools](/wiki/Technical_school "Technical school"). More than nine-tenths of the population are literate and roughly the same proportion of those age 7 to 14 are enrolled in school. There are 578 universities in the state of São Paulo.[[168]](#cite_note-168)

The city of São Paulo is also home to research and development facilities and attracts companies due to the presence of regionally renowned universities. Science, technology and innovation is leveraged by the allocation of funds from the [state government](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_(state) "São Paulo (state)"), mainly carried out by means of the Foundation to Research Support in the State of São Paulo (Fundação de Amparo à Pesquisa do Estado de São Paulo – FAPESP), one of the main agencies promoting scientific and technological research.[[169]](#cite_note-169)

Further information: [Faculdade de Informática e Administração Paulista](/wiki/Faculdade_de_Inform%C3%A1tica_e_Administra%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Paulista "Faculdade de Informática e Administração Paulista")

### Health care

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A3o_Paulo&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Health care")]

Main article: [Health in Brazil](/wiki/Health_in_Brazil "Health in Brazil")

See also: [Rede São Paulo Saudável](/wiki/Rede_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Saud%C3%A1vel "Rede São Paulo Saudável")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Instituto_do_C%C3%A2ncer_de_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Octavio_Frias_de_Oliveira.jpg/250px-Instituto_do_C%C3%A2ncer_de_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Octavio_Frias_de_Oliveira.jpg)](/wiki/File:Instituto_do_C%C3%A2ncer_de_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Octavio_Frias_de_Oliveira.jpg)

[Institute of Cancer of São Paulo](/wiki/Institute_of_Cancer_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Institute of Cancer of São Paulo"), the largest [oncology](/wiki/Oncology "Oncology") center in [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America")[[170]](#cite_note-170)[[171]](#cite_note-171)

São Paulo is one of the largest health care hubs in Latin America. Among its hospitals are the [Albert Einstein Israelite Hospital](/wiki/Albert_Einstein_Israelite_Hospital "Albert Einstein Israelite Hospital"), ranked the best hospital in all Latin America[[172]](#cite_note-172) and the [Hospital das Clínicas](/wiki/Hospital_das_Cl%C3%ADnicas_da_Universidade_de_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Hospital das Clínicas da Universidade de São Paulo"), the largest in the region, with a total area of 600,000 square meters and offers 2,400 beds, distributed among its eight specialized institutes and two assisting hospitals.[[173]](#cite_note-173)

The main hospitals in the city of São Paulo concentrate in the upper-income areas, the majority of the population of the city has a private health insurance. This can includes hospitals, private practices and pharmacies. The city of São Paulo has the largest number of foreigners comparing with any other Brazilian city and an intense [health tourism](/wiki/Health_tourism "Health tourism"). In Brazil, the city of São Paulo has the largest number of doctors who can speak more than one language, which in this case is [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"), with the secondary languages predominantly are [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") and [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language").[[174]](#cite_note-174)

The private health care sector is very large and most of Brazil's best hospitals are in the city. As of September 2009, the city of São Paulo had: 32,553 [ambulatory](/wiki/Ambulatory "Ambulatory") clinics, centers and professional offices (physicians, dentists and others); 217 hospitals, with 32,554 beds; 137,745 health care professionals, including 28,316 physicians.[[175]](#cite_note-175)

The municipal government operates public [health facilities](/wiki/Health_facilities "Health facilities") across the city's territory, with 770 primary health care units (UBS), ambulatory and emergency clinics and 17 hospitals. The Municipal Secretary of Health has 59,000 employees, including 8,000 physicians and 12,000 nurses. 6,000,000 citizens uses the facilities, which provide drugs at no cost and manage an extensive family health program (PSF – Programa de Saúde da Família).[[176]](#cite_note-SIGA_Saúde-176)

The Sistema Integrado de Gestão de Assistência à Saúde de São Paulo – SIGA Saúde (*Integrated Health Care Management System in São Paulo*) has been operating in the city of São Paulo since 2004. Today there are more than 22 million registered users, including the people of the [Greater São Paulo](/wiki/Greater_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Greater São Paulo"), with a monthly average of 1.3 million appointments.[[176]](#cite_note-SIGA_Saúde-176)

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A3o_Paulo&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Government")]

See also: [List of mayors of São Paulo](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "List of mayors of São Paulo")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Matarazzo_Building.jpg/250px-Matarazzo_Building.jpg)](/wiki/File:Matarazzo_Building.jpg)

[Matarazzo Building](/wiki/Matarazzo_Building "Matarazzo Building"), the [São Paulo city hall](/wiki/Seat_of_local_government "Seat of local government")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Pal%C3%A1cio_Anchieta_%28SP%29.jpg/250px-Pal%C3%A1cio_Anchieta_%28SP%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pal%C3%A1cio_Anchieta_(SP).jpg)

[Municipal Chamber of São Paulo](/wiki/Municipal_Chamber_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Municipal Chamber of São Paulo")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Pal%C3%A1cio_dos_Bandeirantes_foto001_%2843944820834%29.jpg/250px-Pal%C3%A1cio_dos_Bandeirantes_foto001_%2843944820834%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pal%C3%A1cio_dos_Bandeirantes_foto001_(43944820834).jpg)

[Bandeirantes Palace](/wiki/Pal%C3%A1cio_dos_Bandeirantes "Palácio dos Bandeirantes"), the seat of state government

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Assembleia_Legislativa_do_Estado_de_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_01.jpg/250px-Assembleia_Legislativa_do_Estado_de_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Assembleia_Legislativa_do_Estado_de_S%C3%A3o_Paulo_01.jpg)

[Legislative Assembly of São Paulo](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Legislative Assembly of São Paulo")

As the capital of the [state of São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_(state) "São Paulo (state)"), the city is home to the [Bandeirantes Palace](/wiki/Pal%C3%A1cio_dos_Bandeirantes "Palácio dos Bandeirantes") (state government) and the [Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Legislative Assembly of São Paulo"). The Executive Branch of the municipality of São Paulo is represented by the [mayor](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor") and his cabinet of secretaries, following the model proposed by the [Federal Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Brazil "Constitution of Brazil").[[177]](#cite_note-177) The organic law of the municipality and the Master Plan of the city, however, determine that the public administration must guarantee to the population effective tools of manifestation of participatory democracy, which causes that the city is divided in regional prefectures, each one led by a Regional Mayor appointed by the Mayor.[[178]](#cite_note-districts-178)

The legislative power is represented by the Municipal Chamber, composed of 55 aldermen elected to four-year posts (in compliance with the provisions of Article 29 of the Constitution, which dictates a minimum number of 42 and a maximum of 55 for municipalities with more than five million inhabitants). It is up to the house to draft and vote fundamental laws for the administration and the Executive, especially the municipal budget (known as the Law of Budgetary Guidelines).[[179]](#cite_note-179) In addition to the legislative process and the work of the secretariats, there are also a number of municipal councils, each dealing with different topics, composed of representatives of the various sectors of organized civil society. The actual performance and representativeness of such councils, however, are sometimes questioned.

The following municipal councils are active: Municipal Council for Children and Adolescents (CMDCA); of Informatics (WCC); of the Physically Disabled (CMDP); of Education (CME); of Housing (CMH); of Environment (CADES); of Health (CMS); of Tourism (COMTUR); of Human Rights (CMDH); of Culture (CMC); and of Social Assistance (COMAS) and Drugs and Alcohol (COMUDA). The Prefecture also owns (or is the majority partner in their social capital) a series of companies responsible for various aspects of public services and the economy of São Paulo:

* [*São Paulo Turismo S/A*](/wiki/Anhembi_Convention_Center "Anhembi Convention Center") (SPTuris): company responsible for organizing large events and promoting the [city's tourism](/wiki/Tourism_in_the_city_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Tourism in the city of São Paulo").
* *Companhia de Engenharia de Tráfego* (CET):[[180]](#cite_note-180) subordinated to the Municipal Transportation Department, is responsible for traffic supervision, fines (in cooperation with DETRAN) and maintenance of the city's road system.
* *Companhia Metropolitana de Habitação de São Paulo* (COHAB): subordinate to the Department of Housing, is responsible for the implementation of public housing policies, especially the construction of housing developments.
* *Empresa Municipal de Urbanização de São Paulo* (EMURB): subordinate to the Planning Department, is responsible for urban works and for the maintenance of public spaces and urban furniture.
* *Companhia de Processamento de Dados de São Paulo* (PRODAM): responsible for the electronic infrastructure and information technology of the city hall.
* [*São Paulo Transportes Sociedade Anônima*](/wiki/SPTrans "SPTrans") (SPTrans): responsible for the operation of the public transport systems managed by the city hall, such as the municipal bus lines.

### Subdivisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A3o_Paulo&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Subdivisions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/SaoPaulo_Municip_SaoPaulo.svg/250px-SaoPaulo_Municip_SaoPaulo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:SaoPaulo_Municip_SaoPaulo.svg)

Location on a map of [municipalities of the state of São Paulo](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "List of municipalities in São Paulo")

São Paulo is divided into 32 [subprefectures](/wiki/Subprefecture "Subprefecture"), each with an administration ("subprefeitura") divided into several districts ("distritos").[[178]](#cite_note-districts-178) The city also has a radial division into nine zones for purpose of traffic control and bus lines, which do not fit into the administrative divisions. These zones are identified by colors in the street signs. The historical core of São Paulo, which includes the inner city and the area of [Paulista Avenue](/wiki/Paulista_Avenue "Paulista Avenue"), is in the [Subprefecture of Sé](/wiki/Subprefecture_of_S%C3%A9 "Subprefecture of Sé"). Most other economic and tourist facilities of the city are inside an area officially called *Centro Expandido* (Portuguese for "Broad Center", or "Broad Downtown"), which includes Sé and several other subprefectures, and areas immediately around it.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A3o_Paulo&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main articles: [Economy of São Paulo](/wiki/Economy_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Economy of São Paulo"), [Economy of the state of São Paulo](/wiki/Economy_of_the_state_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Economy of the state of São Paulo"), and [Economy of Brazil](/wiki/Economy_of_Brazil "Economy of Brazil")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Vista_da_Avenida_Paulista_-_Sesc_Avenida_Paulista_por_Rodrigo_Tetsuo_Argenton_%281%29.jpg/250px-Vista_da_Avenida_Paulista_-_Sesc_Avenida_Paulista_por_Rodrigo_Tetsuo_Argenton_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vista_da_Avenida_Paulista_-_Sesc_Avenida_Paulista_por_Rodrigo_Tetsuo_Argenton_(1).jpg)

[Paulista Avenue](/wiki/Paulista_Avenue "Paulista Avenue"), the headquarters of many financial and cultural institutions

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Avenida_Brigadeiro_Faria_Lima2.webp/250px-Avenida_Brigadeiro_Faria_Lima2.webp.png)](/wiki/File:Avenida_Brigadeiro_Faria_Lima2.webp)

[Brigadeiro Faria Lima Avenue](/wiki/Brigadeiro_Faria_Lima_Avenue "Brigadeiro Faria Lima Avenue"), the main [financial district](/wiki/List_of_financial_districts "List of financial districts") of São Paulo

São Paulo is the city with the largest share of Brazil's GDP and has one of the [highest city GDPs in the world](/wiki/List_of_cities_by_GDP "List of cities by GDP").[[183]](#cite_note-Estadão-183)[[184]](#cite_note-184) According to data from the [IBGE](/wiki/Brazilian_Institute_of_Geography_and_Statistics "Brazilian Institute of Geography and Statistics"), its gross domestic product (GDP) in 2010 was R$450 billion,[[185]](#cite_note-185) approximately US$220 billion, 12.26% of Brazilian GDP and 36% of the São Paulo state's GDP.[[186]](#cite_note-186) The per capita income for the city was R$47.802,02 in 2022.[[187]](#cite_note-187)

São Paulo is considered the [financial capital](/wiki/Financial_capital "Financial capital") of Brazil, as it is the location for the headquarters of major corporations and of banks and financial institutions. The city is the headquarters of [B3](/wiki/B3_(stock_exchange) "B3 (stock exchange)"), the [largest stock exchange of Latin America by market capitalization](/wiki/List_of_major_stock_exchanges "List of major stock exchanges"),[[25]](#cite_note-B3-25) and has several [financial districts](/wiki/List_of_financial_districts "List of financial districts"), mainly in the areas around [Paulista](/wiki/Paulista_Avenue "Paulista Avenue"), [Faria Lima](/wiki/Brigadeiro_Faria_Lima_Avenue "Brigadeiro Faria Lima Avenue") and [Berrini](/wiki/Engenheiro_Lu%C3%ADs_Carlos_Berrini_Avenue "Engenheiro Luís Carlos Berrini Avenue") avenues. 63% of all the [international companies](/wiki/Company "Company") with business in Brazil have their head offices in São Paulo. São Paulo has one of the largest concentrations of German businesses worldwide[[188]](#cite_note-188) and is the largest Swedish industrial hub alongside [Gothenburg](/wiki/Gothenburg "Gothenburg").[[189]](#cite_note-189)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Sao_Paulo_Stock_Exchange.jpg/250px-Sao_Paulo_Stock_Exchange.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sao_Paulo_Stock_Exchange.jpg)

[B3](/wiki/B3_(stock_exchange) "B3 (stock exchange)"), the [largest stock exchange of Latin America by market capitalization](/wiki/List_of_major_stock_exchanges "List of major stock exchanges")[[190]](#cite_note-190)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Marginal_Pinheiros.png/250px-S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Marginal_Pinheiros.png)](/wiki/File:S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Marginal_Pinheiros.png)

[United Nations Business Center](/wiki/Centro_Empresarial_Na%C3%A7%C3%B5es_Unidas "Centro Empresarial Nações Unidas") at [Brooklin Novo](/wiki/Brooklin_Novo "Brooklin Novo")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Rua_Oscar_Freire.jpg/250px-Rua_Oscar_Freire.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rua_Oscar_Freire.jpg)

[Oscar Freire St.](/wiki/Rua_Oscar_Freire "Rua Oscar Freire"), one of the most luxurious streets in the world[[191]](#cite_note-191)

As of 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A3o_Paulo&action=edit), São Paulo is the third largest exporting municipality in Brazil after [Parauapebas, PA](/wiki/Parauapebas "Parauapebas") and [Rio de Janeiro, RJ](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"). In that year São Paulo's exported goods totaled $7.32B (USD) or 3.02% of Brazil's total exports. The top five [commodities](/wiki/Commodities "Commodities") exported by São Paulo are [soybean](/wiki/Soybean "Soybean") (21%), [raw sugar](/wiki/Sugar "Sugar") (19%), [coffee](/wiki/Coffee "Coffee") (6.5%), [sulfate chemical wood pulp](/wiki/Pulp_(paper) "Pulp (paper)") (5.6%), and [corn](/wiki/Maize "Maize") (4.4%).[[192]](#cite_note-192)

São Paulo's economy is going through a deep transformation. Once a city with a strong [industrial character](/wiki/Secondary_sector_of_the_economy "Secondary sector of the economy"), São Paulo's economy has followed the global trend of shifting to the [tertiary sector of the economy](/wiki/Tertiary_sector_of_the_economy "Tertiary sector of the economy"), focusing on services. São Paulo also has a large "informal" economy.[[193]](#cite_note-193) According to [PricewaterhouseCoopers](/wiki/PricewaterhouseCoopers "PricewaterhouseCoopers") average annual economic growth of the city is 4.2%.[[194]](#cite_note-194) In 2005, the city of São Paulo collected R$90 billion in taxes and the city budget was R$15 billion. The city has 1,500 bank branches and 70 shopping malls.[[195]](#cite_note-195)

The city is unique among Brazilian cities for its large number of foreign corporations.[[196]](#cite_note-196) São Paulo ranked second after New York in [FDi magazine](/wiki/FDi_magazine "FDi magazine")'s bi-annual ranking of Cities of the Future 2013–14 in the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"), and was named the Latin American City of the Future 2013–14.[[197]](#cite_note-197) According to [Mercer](/wiki/Mercer_(consulting_firm) "Mercer (consulting firm)")'s 2011 [city rankings of cost of living for expatriate employees](/wiki/List_of_most_expensive_cities_for_expatriate_employees#Mercer_surveys "List of most expensive cities for expatriate employees"), São Paulo is among the ten most expensive cities in the world.[[198]](#cite_note-198)[[199]](#cite_note-199)

[Luxury brands](/wiki/Luxury_brand "Luxury brand") tend to concentrate their business in São Paulo. Because of the lack of department stores and multi-brand boutiques, [shopping malls](/wiki/Shopping_mall "Shopping mall") as well as the [Jardins](/wiki/Jardins "Jardins") district attract most of the world's luxurious brands. Most of the international luxury brands can be found in the [Iguatemi](/wiki/Iguatemi_Empresa_de_Shopping_Centers "Iguatemi Empresa de Shopping Centers"), Cidade Jardim or JK shopping malls or on the streets of [Oscar Freire](/wiki/Rua_Oscar_Freire "Rua Oscar Freire"), Lorena or Haddock Lobo in the Jardins district. They are home of brands such as [Cartier](/wiki/Cartier_(jeweler) "Cartier (jeweler)"), [Chanel](/wiki/Chanel "Chanel"), [Dior](/wiki/Dior "Dior"), [Giorgio Armani](/wiki/Giorgio_Armani "Giorgio Armani"), [Gucci](/wiki/Gucci "Gucci"), [Louis Vuitton](/wiki/Louis_Vuitton "Louis Vuitton"), [Marc Jacobs](/wiki/Marc_Jacobs "Marc Jacobs"), [Tiffany & Co](/wiki/Tiffany_%26_Co "Tiffany & Co"). Cidade Jardim was opened in São Paulo in 2008, it is a 45,000-square-meter (484,376-square-foot) mall, landscaped with trees and greenery scenario, with a focus on Brazilian brands but also home to international luxury brands such as [Hermès](/wiki/Herm%C3%A8s "Hermès"), [Jimmy Choo](/wiki/Jimmy_Choo "Jimmy Choo"), [Pucci](/wiki/Emilio_Pucci "Emilio Pucci") and [Carolina Herrera](/wiki/Carolina_Herrera_(fashion_designer) "Carolina Herrera (fashion designer)"). Opened in 2012, JK shopping mall has brought to Brazil brands that were not present in the country before such as Goyard, Tory Burch, Llc., [Prada](/wiki/Prada "Prada"), and Miu Miu.[[200]](#cite_note-200)

The [Iguatemi](/wiki/Iguatemi_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Iguatemi São Paulo") Faria Lima, in [Faria Lima Avenue](/wiki/Avenida_Brigadeiro_Faria_Lima "Avenida Brigadeiro Faria Lima"), is Brazil's oldest mall, opened in 1966.[[201]](#cite_note-201) The [Jardins](/wiki/Jardins "Jardins") neighborhood is regarded among the most sophisticated places in town, with upscale restaurants and hotels. The New York Times once compared Oscar Freire Street to [Rodeo Drive](/wiki/Rodeo_Drive "Rodeo Drive").[[202]](#cite_note-202) In Jardins there are luxury car dealers. One of the world's best restaurants as elected by The World's 50 Best Restaurants Award, D.O.M.,[[203]](#cite_note-203) is there.

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A3o_Paulo&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Tourism")]

Main article: [Tourism in the city of São Paulo](/wiki/Tourism_in_the_city_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Tourism in the city of São Paulo")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Parque_do_ibirapuera_visto_do_c%C3%A9u.jpg/250px-Parque_do_ibirapuera_visto_do_c%C3%A9u.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parque_do_ibirapuera_visto_do_c%C3%A9u.jpg)

[Ibirapuera Park](/wiki/Ibirapuera_Park "Ibirapuera Park"), elected the best in South America by [TripAdvisor](/wiki/TripAdvisor "TripAdvisor")[[204]](#cite_note-204)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Inaugura%C3%A7%C3%A3o_da_Roda_Gigante_%2852551751466%29.jpg/250px-Inaugura%C3%A7%C3%A3o_da_Roda_Gigante_%2852551751466%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Inaugura%C3%A7%C3%A3o_da_Roda_Gigante_(52551751466).jpg)

[Roda Rico](/wiki/Roda_Rico "Roda Rico"), the tallest [observation wheel](/wiki/Observation_wheel "Observation wheel") in Latin America[[205]](#cite_note-Estrutura-205)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Anhangaba%C3%BA_de_dia.jpg/250px-Anhangaba%C3%BA_de_dia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anhangaba%C3%BA_de_dia.jpg)

[Anhangabaú Valley](/wiki/Anhangaba%C3%BA_Valley "Anhangabaú Valley") in the [Historic Center](/wiki/Historic_Center_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Historic Center of São Paulo")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Itaim_Bibi_Business_District.jpg/250px-Itaim_Bibi_Business_District.jpg)](/wiki/File:Itaim_Bibi_Business_District.jpg)

[People's Park](/wiki/People%27s_Park_(S%C3%A3o_Paulo) "People's Park (São Paulo)") in [Itaim Bibi](/wiki/Itaim_Bibi "Itaim Bibi")

Large hotel chains whose target audience is the corporate traveler are in the city. São Paulo is home to 75% of the country's leading business fairs. The city also promotes one of the most important fashion weeks in the world, [São Paulo Fashion Week](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Fashion_Week "São Paulo Fashion Week"), established in 1996 under the name Morumbi Fashion Brasil, is the largest and most important fashion event in Latin America.[[206]](#cite_note-206) Besides, the [São Paulo Gay Pride Parade](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Gay_Pride_Parade "São Paulo Gay Pride Parade"), held since 1997 on [Paulista Avenue](/wiki/Paulista_Avenue "Paulista Avenue") is the event that attracts more tourists to the city.[[207]](#cite_note-207)

The annual March For Jesus is a large gathering of Christians from Protestant churches throughout Brazil, with São Paulo police reporting participation in the range of 350,000 in 2015.[[208]](#cite_note-208) In addition, São Paulo hosts the annual São Paulo Pancake Cook-Off in which chefs from across Brazil and the world participate in competitions based on the cooking of [pancakes](/wiki/Pancakes "Pancakes").[[209]](#cite_note-209)

São Paulo has been named the 18th best city in the world by the 2026 World's Best Cities Report, standing out among 270 global destinations evaluated by Resonance. The city leads all of Latin America, ahead of Mexico City, Buenos Aires, Rio de Janeiro, Bogotá and Lima.[[210]](#cite_note-210)

The city has a nightlife that is considered one of the best in the country, and is an international hub of highly active and diverse [nightlife](/wiki/Nightlife "Nightlife") with [bars](/wiki/Bar_(establishment) "Bar (establishment)"), dance bars and [nightclubs](/wiki/Nightclub "Nightclub") staying open well past midnight.[[211]](#cite_note-211) There are cinemas, theaters, museums, and cultural centers. The [Rua Oscar Freire](/wiki/Rua_Oscar_Freire "Rua Oscar Freire") was named one of the eight most luxurious streets in the world, according to the Mystery Shopping International,[[212]](#cite_note-Oscar_Freire-212) and São Paulo the 25th "most expensive city" of the planet.[[213]](#cite_note-213)

According to the International Congress & Convention Association, São Paulo ranks first among the cities that host international events in [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas") and the 12th in the world, after [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna"), [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"), [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona"), [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"), [Budapest](/wiki/Budapest "Budapest"), [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam"), [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm"), [Seoul](/wiki/Seoul "Seoul"), [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon "Lisbon"), and [Copenhagen](/wiki/Copenhagen "Copenhagen").[[214]](#cite_note-214)

According to a study by [MasterCard](/wiki/MasterCard "MasterCard") in 130 cities around the world, São Paulo was the third most visited destination in Latin America (behind [Mexico City](/wiki/Mexico_City "Mexico City") and [Buenos Aires](/wiki/Buenos_Aires "Buenos Aires")) with 2.4 million foreign travelers, who spent US$2.9 billion in 2013 (the highest among the cities in the region). In 2014, [CNN](/wiki/CNN "CNN") ranked nightlife São Paulo as the fourth best in the world, behind [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin") and [Ibiza](/wiki/Ibiza "Ibiza"), in [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain").[[215]](#cite_note-215)

The cuisine of the region is a tourist attraction. The city has 62 cuisines across 12,000 restaurants.[[216]](#cite_note-216) During the 10th International Congress of Gastronomy, Hospitality and Tourism (Cihat) conducted in 1997, the city received the title of "World Gastronomy Capital" from a commission formed by 43 nations' representatives.[[217]](#cite_note-217)

## Urban infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S%C3%A3o_Paulo&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Urban infrastructure")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/SP_from_Altino_Arantes_Building.jpg/250px-SP_from_Altino_Arantes_Building.jpg)](/wiki/File:SP_from_Altino_Arantes_Building.jpg)

The city view from [Altino Arantes Building](/wiki/Altino_Arantes_Building "Altino Arantes Building")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Brazil_12Mar2018_SkySat.jpg/250px-S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Brazil_12Mar2018_SkySat.jpg)](/wiki/File:S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Brazil_12Mar2018_SkySat.jpg)

Changes in urban fabrics in the region of [Jardins](/wiki/Jardins "Jardins"): side by side, vertical areas and low houses

Since the beginning of the 20th century, São Paulo has been one of the main economic centers of Latin America. With the [First](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") and [Second World Wars](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") and the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"), coffee exports to the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") were heavily affected, forcing the rich coffee growers to invest in the industrial activities that would make São Paulo the largest industrial center in Brazil. The new job vacancies contributed to attract a significant number of immigrants (mainly from Italy)[[218]](#cite_note-Italian-218) and migrants, especially from the Northeastern states.[[219]](#cite_note-Causas-219) From a population of only 32.000 people in 1880, São Paulo now had 8.5 million inhabitants in 1980. The rapid population growth has brought many problems for the city.

São Paulo is practically all served by the water supply network. The city consumes an average of 221 liters of water/inhabitant/day while the UN recommends the consumption of 110 liters/day [Sources?]. The water loss is 30.8%. However, between 11 and 12.8% of households do not have a sewage system, depositing waste in pits and ditches. Sixty percent of the sewage collected is treated. According to data from IBGE and Eletropaulo, the electricity grid serves almost 100% of households. The fixed telephony network is still precarious, with coverage of 67.2%. Household garbage collection covers all regions of the municipality but is still insufficient, reaching around 94% of the demand in districts such as [Parelheiros](/wiki/Parelheiros_(district_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo) "Parelheiros (district of São Paulo)") and Perus. About 80% of the garbage produced daily by Paulistas is exported to other cities, such as [Caieiras](/wiki/Caieiras "Caieiras") and [Guarulhos](/wiki/Guarulhos "Guarulhos").[[220]](#cite_note-lixo-220) Recycling accounts for about 1% of the 15,000 metric tons of waste produced daily.[[220]](#cite_note-lixo-220)

[Marginal Tietê](/wiki/Marginal_Tiet%C3%AA "Marginal Tietê"), the main [expressway](/wiki/Controlled-access_highway "Controlled-access highway") of the city

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Chucri_Zaidan_Pinheiros_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Chucri_Zaidan_Pinheiros_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chucri_Zaidan_Pinheiros_-_panoramio.jpg)

[Marginal Pinheiros](/wiki/Marginal_Pinheiros "Marginal Pinheiros")

The original nuclei of the city are vertical, characterized by the presence of commercial buildings and services. The peripheries are generally developed with two to four-story buildings, though there are exceptions. Compared to other global cities (such as the island cities of [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") and [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong")), however, São Paulo is considered a "low-rise building" city. Its tallest buildings rarely reach forty stories, and the average residential building has twenty. Nevertheless, it is the fourth city in the world in quantity of buildings,[[221]](#cite_note-221) besides possessing what was considered until 2014 the tallest skyscraper of the country, the [Mirante do Vale](/wiki/Mirante_do_Vale "Mirante do Vale"), also known as *Palácio Zarzur Kogan*, with 170 meters. Currently the tallest building in the city is [Alto Das Nações](/wiki/Alto_das_Na%C3%A7%C3%B5es "Alto das Nações") at 219 meters [[222]](#cite_note-222)

Some central regions of the city began to concentrate indigents, [drug trafficking](/wiki/Drug_trafficking "Drug trafficking"), street vending and [prostitution](/wiki/Prostitution "Prostitution"), which encouraged the creation of new socio-economic centralities. The characterization of each region of the city also underwent several changes throughout the 20th century. With the relocation of industries to other cities or states, several areas that once housed factory sheds have become commercial or even residential areas.[[223]](#cite_note-Planejamento-223)

São Paulo has a history of actions, projects and plans related to urban planning that can be traced to the governments of Antonio da Silva Prado, Baron Duprat, Washington and Luis Francisco Prestes Maia. However, in general, the city was formed during the 20th century, growing from village to metropolis through a series of informal processes and irregular urban sprawl.[[224]](#cite_note-224)

Urban growth in São Paulo has followed three patterns since the beginning of the 20th century, according to urban historians: since the late 19th century and until the 1940s, São Paulo was a condensed city in which different social groups lived in a small urban zone separated by type of housing; from the 1940s to the 1980s, São Paulo followed a model of center-periphery social segregation, in which the upper and middle-classes occupied central and modern areas while the poor moved towards precarious, self-built housing in the periphery; and from the 1980s onward, new transformations have brought the social classes closer together in spatial terms, but separated by walls and security technologies that seek to isolate the richer classes in the name of security.[[225]](#cite_note-Caldeira,_Teresa_P.R._2000._p._215-225) Thus, São Paulo differs considerably from other Brazilian cities such as [Belo Horizonte](/wiki/Belo_Horizonte "Belo Horizonte") and [Goiânia](/wiki/Goi%C3%A2nia "Goiânia"), whose initial expansion followed determinations by a plan, or a city like [Brasília](/wiki/Bras%C3%ADlia "Brasília"), whose master plan had been fully developed prior to construction.[[226]](#cite_note-226)

The effectiveness of these plans has been seen by some planners and historians as questionable. Some of these scholars argue that such plans were produced exclusively for the benefit of the wealthier strata of the population while the working classes would be relegated to the traditional informal processes. In São Paulo until the mid-1950s, the plans were based on the idea of "demolish and rebuild", including former Mayor [Francisco Prestes Maia](/wiki/Francisco_Prestes_Maia "Francisco Prestes Maia")'s road plan for São Paulo (known as the Avenues Plan) or [Saturnino de Brito](/wiki/Saturnino_de_Brito "Saturnino de Brito")'s plan for the Tietê River. The Plan of the Avenues was implemented during the 1920s and sought to build large avenues connecting the city center with the outskirts. This plan included renewing the commercial city center, leading to real estate speculation and gentrification of several downtown neighborhoods. The plan also led to the expansion of bus services, which would soon replace the trolley as the preliminary transportation system.[[227]](#cite_note-227) This contributed to the outwards expansion of São Paulo and the peripherization of poorer residents. Peripheral neighborhoods were usually unregulated and consisted mainly of self-built single-family houses.[[225]](#cite_note-Caldeira,_Teresa_P.R._2000._p._215-225)

In 1968 the Urban Development Plan proposed the Basic Plan for Integrated Development of São Paulo, under the administration of Figueiredo Ferraz. The main result was zoning laws. It lasted until 2004 when the Basic Plan was replaced by the current Master Plan.[[228]](#cite_note-228) That [zoning](/wiki/Zoning_in_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Zoning in São Paulo"), adopted in 1972, designated "Z1" areas ([residential areas](/wiki/Residential_area "Residential area") designed for elites) and "Z3" (a "mixed zone" lacking clear definitions about their characteristics). Zoning encouraged the growth of suburbs with minimal control and major speculation.[[229]](#cite_note-229) After the 1970s peripheral lot regulation increased and infrastructure in the periphery improved, driving land prices up. The poorest and the newcomers now could not purchase their lot and build their house, and were forced to look for a housing alternative. As a result, [favelas](/wiki/Favela "Favela") and precarious tenements (cortiços) appeared.[[230]](#cite_note-230) These housing types were often closer to the city's center: favelas could sprawl in any unused terrain (often dangerous or unsanitary) and decaying or abandoned buildings for tenements were abundant inside the city. Favelas went back into the urban perimeter, occupying the small lots not yet occupied by urbanization – alongside polluted rivers, railways, or between bridges.[[231]](#cite_note-231) By 1993, 19.8% of São Paulo's population lived in [favelas](/wiki/Favelas "Favelas"), compared to 5.2% in 1980.[[232]](#cite_note-232) Today, it is estimated that 2.1 million Paulistas live in favelas, which represents about 11% of the metropolitan area's population.[[233]](#cite_note-233)
